experiments.title: How defaulting a form to monthly & adding a monthly callout affects donor conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that defaulting the donation form to Monthly giving and adding a clear, impact-focused callout for users on acquisition offer donation pages will achieve an increase in monthly donor conversion because default bias nudges people toward the pre-selected choice, while a tangible impact message increases perceived efficacy and reduces decision friction—making monthly giving feel both easier and more meaningful.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/19/2025 - 08/29/2025
Focus on the Family (FOTF) uses free video series offers, promoted primarily on Facebook and Instagram, to acquire new subscribers and donors. These campaigns have recently become highly efficient at driving new support, as optimizations significantly lowered the gross cost per donor (GCPD) and increased the volume of one-time donors acquired.
With this success, the team sought to improve the quality of donor acquisition by increasing the number of recurring donors acquired through this same channel. The key question: could making monthly giving more visually and textually prominent on the donation form encourage more people to begin a recurring commitment at the point of acquisition?
The Control used the standard acquisition donation form, which defaulted to a $30 one-time gift and contained no additional monthly-specific messaging.
The Treatment donation form was identical in design and copy but defaulted to $30 monthly giving. It also added a callout above the form that read:
“Would you consider a monthly donation? Your monthly gift of $30 will enable 50 people every month to get signed up for this free video series.”
The test ran across all active video series acquisition donation pages for 10 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Defaulting the donation form to Monthly and adding an impact-focused callout led to a 187.7% increase in monthly gifts (99.9% confidence), showing that defaults and impact framing can strongly influence recurring donor acquisition. The average gift also increased slightly ($33 → $36), though not with statistical confidence.
At the same time, one-time gifts decreased by 34.2% (99.9% confidence). However, this is because many of the people who would have given a one-time donation chose to give a monthly gift instead.
The takeaway: monthly defaults can meaningfully grow recurring donors but risk suppressing one-time gifts. Whether this is a net positive depends on the lifetime value of new monthly donors compared to the lost one-time gifts.
Future tests could evaluate long-term revenue impact, try highlighting monthly giving without changing the default, or explore post-donation monthly upgrade prompts.

experiments.title: What is the better donation cta for the hero image?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that swapping the control doantion cta with an Adventures in Odyssey doantion ask for the homepage hero banner will achieve more transactions because more people will be familiar with AIO.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/05/2025
The ministry itself spans through several different mediums, such as podcasts, books, as well as children’s programming such as Adventures in Odyssey.
For this test we wanted to see if the current donation call to action (cta)  for Raising Highly Capable Kids on the homepage slider is the best option to show when people who are coming to the site might not be familiar with the organization. We swapped the Highly Capable Kids slide with the Adventures in Odyssey slide.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that swapping the donation call to action for Adventures in Odyssey in the homepage slider resulted in a significant increase in donations for all traffic, with a 22.2% improvement and a statistical confidence level of 96.3%. This outcome suggests that featuring Adventures in Odyssey, a more familiar and well-known program within the organization, resonated better with visitors and encouraged more donations.

In future experimentation, we should consider the familiarity and popularity of different programs or campaigns when determining which call to action to feature prominently. By prioritizing content that is already well-known and loved by our audience, we can potentially increase engagement and drive more conversions. Additionally, we should continue to track and analyze the results of A/B tests to gather insights and optimize our strategies for maximizing donations and achieving our organizational goals.

experiments.title: How a reducing the gift array quantity impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing some donation amounts from the gift array for web visitors that hit the donation page will achieve a higher number of transactions because by removing the number of options a prospective donor has, there will not be as much donor friction.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/30/2023
Focus on the Family is a widely known global ministry. For this experiment, we wanted to see if we could decrease donor friction by removing some donation amounts. By removing some gift options and making the donor have to think less about the gift amount, we thought that that would increase the total number of conversions.
However, this test proved us wrong and is a great example of why we test things and not just roll it out. By removing gift options we negatively impacted the number of conversions and total revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that removing donation amounts from the gift array for web visitors negatively impacted the number of donations and total revenue. Contrary to our hypothesis, this change led to a decrease in conversions, indicating that providing more options to prospective donors is actually beneficial.

Having less gift arrays is a standard best practice, we believe that this could have caused friction because 1. we did not include the "other" button where a donor could type in a gift that is less than 50. The team will need to dig into the average gift a little more and then retest this.

experiments.title: How a simplified product landing page that leads with value proposition impacts clicks to purchase
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a simplified product landing page that leads with value proposition will increase clicks to purchase by increasing the perceived value of the product
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/21/2023 - 12/05/2023
An organization was launching a brand new streaming series called RVL Discipleship: The Study. Unlike their traditional video series’ acquisition offers that are free with an instant donation opportunity, this would be a product sold through the Focus on the Family store.
The funnel at launch included a product landing page that pointed to the store page that pointed to the cart and checkout pages. The primary purpose of the product landing page was to increase interest in the streaming series and get visitors to click the button to purchase the series.
In reviewing the page, our team noticed that it led with a promo trailer and then offered a free episode. While there was some value proposition copy on the page, it was further down the page and overall, the user experience was very different than the experience of someone on one of their video series’ landing pages that we’ve refined through testing during the last three years. Instead of focusing on the product, there seemed to be almost as much time focused on explaining who the Ray Vander Laan was. We hypothesized that a treatment page that looked more like their video series’ landing pages, with a clear value-driven headline at the top and copy explaining the impact of the series would lead to more purchase clicks than the current design because it would provide greater clarity about the product and its impact on buyers.
We designed a new version of the page and used VWO to split traffic between both versions. We measured clicks to purchase on each and ran the experiment for about 2.5 weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. Simplifying the product landing page and leading with a clear value proposition can increase clicks to purchase.

2. The treatment page had a 47.2% increase in visits to the RVL Store Page for all traffic.

3. The statistical level of confidence for this increase was 100.0%, indicating that the results are highly reliable.

4. By emphasizing the value and impact of the product, potential buyers are more likely to be interested and motivated to make a purchase.

Our next experiment will be removing the store page and cart pages from the funnel altogether so that visitors to the new landing page who click to purchase go straight to the store checkout page. We believe this will further remove friction and lead to more purchases of the product.

We did see 13 purchases on the treatment page while only 10 on the control, but our level of confidence for this increase in purchases was only 50%. More time and data would be needed to validate the impact on purchases.

experiments.title: How emphasizing the impact of a content offer on a landing page impacts downstream donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that emphasizing the impact the videos will have on subscribers will increase downstream donor conversion by increasing the perceived value of the videos.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/09/2023 - 06/23/2023
Focus on the Family uses dozens of different free video series offers to acquire new subscribers and donors. For most of these offers, their landing pages feature bullet points outlining the content of each episode in the series. In reviewing this standard design, we wanted to see if we could increase the appeal of the videos by using these bullet points to instead clarify what subscribers would be able to do as a result of these videos. This was inspired by Amy Harrison’s copywriting keynote at the 2022 NIO Summit.
We created a treatment version of their Bringing Biblical Truth to a Broken Culture video series landing page where the bullet points stated what subscribers would now be able to do, because of these short videos. We split traffic 50/50 and ran the experiment for 2 weeks. We believe that this new language would increase the perceived value of the videos, leading to more signups on the page and a higher downstream donor conversion on the subsequent donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the new language almost validated an increase in emails acquired on the page, it led to a significant decrease in downstream donor conversion on the subsequent donation page. These results conflicted with the results of a similar test we conducted over the same time period on their Winning Your Inner Battles landing page.

It is possible that the new impact language, while making the offer more appealing in general failed to increase the perceived value for the visitors most likely to donate. So, for these subscribers, the original language was better as it left them with the perceived impact they were looking for in the video series that was removed by the new bullet points. More testing is needed to identify the best impact/value language for this offer to increase donor conversion.

experiments.title: How emphasizing the impact of a content offer on a landing page impacts downstream donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that emphasizing the impact the videos will have on subscribers will increase downstream donor conversion by increasing the perceived value of the videos.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/13/2023 - 06/30/2023
Focus on the Family uses dozens of different free video series offers to acquire new subscribers and donors. For most of these offers, their landing pages feature bullet points outlining the content of each episode in the series. In reviewing this standard design, we wanted to see if we could increase the appeal of the videos by using these bullet points to instead clarify what subscribers would be able to do as a result of these videos. This was inspired by Amy Harrison’s copywriting keynote at the 2022 NIO Summit.
We created a treatment version of their Winning Your Inner Battles video series landing page where the bullet points stated what subscribers would now be able to do, because of these short videos. We split traffic 50/50 and ran the experiment for 2 weeks. We believe that this new language would increase the perceived value of the videos, leading to more signups on the page and a higher downstream donor conversion on the subsequent donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We found that the new language actually decreased signups on the page, however it significantly increased downstream donor conversion. Since we are optimizing this acquisition funnel for donations acquired, this was a clear win for the treatment. We believe that we had fewer subscribers on the treatment because in clarifying what the videos would do, we inevitably excluded some visitors from the page who weren't actually interested in the impact we were promising.  This narrowed the field of possible subscribers. But, it also increased the perceived value of the videos to this newly narrowed audience, which led to a much higher donor conversion on the subsequent donation page. Someone coming to the page wanting these results, wanting to experience daily joy in their walk with God, gave more readily when asked because they believed these videos were going to enable them to do that.
These results also remind us that some tactics will increase one conversion metric but decrease another. That is why it is important to consider the full impact of an experiment on each conversion in the funnel.

experiments.title: How a clearer value proposition on an acquisition offer landing page impacts downstream donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a clearer value proposition on a video series landing page will increase email and donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/24/2023 - 06/16/2023
Focus on the Family is a prominent global ministry that provides video content resources and insights for parents and families on topics such as faith, marriage, and parenting.
In reviewing their Inner Battles Series we felt that the headline and body copy lacked clarity about the battle the series would help subscribers win. “battle with yourself” was too vague. We proposed copy that would emphasize that this series was for anyone facing a “secret struggle with sin” and struggling with “habits, regrets, and sin” holding them back from the victory that “Jesus wants for them.”
We split the traffic and ran the test. Our hypothesis was this could increase landing page donor conversion and subsequent donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The clearer value proposition did not increase email signups. However, in providing a stronger value for subscribers, it increased their likliehood of donating on the subsequent donation page. We believe that people were better able to identify with the promised benefits of the video series, especially those who were actively carrying "shame or regrets" regarding a "secret struggle with sin." This made them more likely to give as they felt seen and cared for.

Our final LOC was between 87%-95%. We had an error where another experiment was launched in the last 3 days of the campaign on the donation page for this offer that would have impacted donor conversion in this experiment. However, in looking at our results prior to that second test launching, we were seeing increased donations for the treatment experience.

We will next test bullet points outlining the impact of these videos rather than simply their content.

experiments.title: How a value driven express giving headline and giving options impacts donor conversion and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that providing common express pay options with a value-driven headline will increase donor conversion and revenue because it will reduce the confusion and friction that users feel around the express giving options.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/19/2023 - 05/06/2023
Focus on the Family (FOTF) is a global Christian ministry that receives a lot of web traffic through its acquisition video series. We previously ran an A/B test on their video series donation pages to test into a new express-giving option that was added to their donation platform. In that test, half of the visitors saw a donation form without an express giving option. The other half of visitors saw the same form with an added express giving section where they could complete their donation using their Anedot login or Apple/Google Pay options.
The results of that test showed that the express giving version resulted in a significant increase in donations, revenue, and conversion rate. We do not believe this indicates that express giving options themselves are the problem, but rather the options used and the way they were presented to users. Simply saying “express checkout” on the form leaves the user with more questions than answers as to how these options benefit them. Moreover, the test used Anedot pay as an option, which was likely too unknown to provide value to the user.
The purpose of this experiment is to test whether presenting just Apple/Google Pay with a value-driven headline will increase the clarity and reduce donation friction for users as they fill out the form. Our hypothesis is that removing the Anedot option and providing extra value language around the express giving option will increase the rates of donor conversion for these acquisition donation pages. We also believe that mobile users will show a higher conversion rate because the express pay options will make it easier to pay while they are on their phones.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that removing the Anedot option and presenting only Apple/Google Pay with a value-driven headline did not increase the rates of donor conversion for the Focus on the Family's video series donation pages. On the contrary, the express giving option still resulted in a significant decrease in donations, revenue, and conversion rate for all traffic when compared to the form without the express giving options. However, one interesting takeaway from this experiment is how Desktop and Mobile users responded to the express giving treatment:
Desktop users saw a 58.5% increase in donations (95.8% LOC), with 70.7% more revenue (98.4% LOC) from the express giving experienceHowever, mobile users made 27.7% fewer donations (99.9% LOC) and gave less money overall, which led to 26.6% less revenue (99.7% LOC)
In contrast to our expectations, the express giving options did not improve the experience for mobile users - it made them significantly less likely to donate. Our analysis is that the express giving option lengthened the form length for mobile users and did not provide enough clarity or value to their donation experience to lift donor conversion or revenue. However, the express giving option led to statistically significant lifts for desktop users, which could be a result of users feeling that the express pay options made it easier for them to donate while at their computer.
Because of these results, we cannot assume that the addition of these express giving options in any form will always lead to an increase in donations. We should carefully consider factors such as the options used, the audience targeted, and the value proposition presented. Further testing would be required to identify whether the placement of the express giving option is contributing to the perceived friction (ex: could placing the express giving option in the Step 2 or Step 3 sections of the form add clarity?). Future tests can also try different value-driven headlines to determine which statements will provide the most clarity and value to users. It would also be worthwhile to test express giving on other donor platforms to see if they have similar results.

experiments.title: How asking for less money on a faith premium offer donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that decreasing the the donation amount to get the premium for people who have just signed up for a video series course will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/19/2023 - 05/08/2023
Focus on the Family is a global ministry that offers video series on topics such as faith, marriage, and parenting.
This is the second test that we have done in this series of lowering the donation amount needed to receive the premium offer. The first test we did was with Cherish Your Spouse, you can find the write-up for it here. We saw a significant impact on donor conversion and revenue by lowering the donation ask to 30 from 50, we wanted to see if that held true across all verticals of Focus’s acquisition offers.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that decreasing the donation amount to get the premium for people who have just signed up for a video series course results in a higher donor conversion rate. The treatment group had a 46.5% increase in donations for all traffic with a 99.1% level of statistical confidence compared to the control group.
The experiment also revealed that mobile, new, and tablet visitors had significant increases in donations, indicating that the treatment was effective across different segments. Mobile visitors had a 408% increase in donations, new visitors had a 534% increase, and tablet visitors had a 2527% increase.
Based on this result and the results of the other offers where a lower appeal amount was tested, we will use a lower donation amount for the time being.

experiments.title: How bringing clarity to a call-to-action impacts donor conversion

rows.name: Get Your Resource for a Gift of Any Amount Here
For a Gift of Any Amount, Get Your Resource Here

experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase donor conversion by bringing clarity to the CTA?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/23/2018 -
On Focus on the Family’s broadcast pages, they offer a daily resource for each broadcast. The button below the resource said, “Give Now”. In a previous experiment we tested that button copy against copy that communicated what people would get when they would click the button. We used button copy that read: “Get the DVD Here”. We were able to significantly increase traffic to the donation page with this tactic, but we actually decreased donor conversion. We hypothesized that with this approach people were expecting to just get their resource by clicking the resource button on that broadcast page and abandoned the donation page once they realized that in order to get the resource they had to make a gift to Focus. To build upon these learnings, we proposed bringing clarity to the buttons rather than the donation page so that we set the expectation prior to them getting to the donation page. To do this we ran a three way test: “Give Now” was the control, “Get Your Resource For a Gift of Any Amount Here” was the first treatment and “For a Gift of Any Amount, Get your Resource Here” was the second treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings: Both treatment versions increased donor conversion! But the first treatment, "Get Your Resource for a Gift of Any Amount Here" increased donor conversion the greatest with 98.9% validity by 18%! When we communicated not only what a person would receive, but also how they would receive it early on in the conversion process, we were able to decrease friction and bring clarity to the conversion process.

experiments.title: How increasing the urgency of a banner appeal  during a high urgency campaign affects recurring donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding urgency to a banner appeal during a high-urgency campaign will increase recurring donor rates by more clearly expressing the need for the prospect to respond to the appeal and to do so now.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/15/2023 - 03/30/2023
We wanted to know how adding urgency to a banner appeal that has traditionally been run during high-urgency campaigns would impact recurring donor rates. The control presents an opportunity to begin your donation on the banner but is supported by generic copy that lacks urgency and does not communicate why someone should give and why they should give now.
We wanted to strengthen the appeal with a treatment that conveys the urgency of the problem in the copy and supports that urgency with a dynamic countdown timer that informs visitors how many monthly donors were still needed to meet the goal … and thus confront the problem.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 12 days, we were able to validate our hypothesis that adding urgency to a banner appeal during a high-urgency campaign would increase recurring donations, producing a 34.6% lift (with a 97% level of confidence) over the control.
For mobile visitors, which made up 72% of total users who entered this experiment, we observed a 66% lift in recurring gifts, with a 99% level of confidence.
Over an 11-month period, with outlier gifts over $1,000 removed, the treatment will produce roughly $13,000 additional revenue, showing the importance of using strong, urgent copy during fundraising campaigns—even in supporting elements outside of appeal emails.
We believe that by calling out the problem and indicating how many more gifts were still needed to hit the goal, we were able to make the appeal more tangible and urgent in the minds of potential donors.
Going forward, we will suggest the high-urgency approach as the control and may consider other ways to improve the appeal, such as highlighting a more specific impact or outcomes achieved from the donor's contribution.

experiments.title: How Removing Express Checkout Options from an Acquisition Donation Page impacts Donor Conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the express giving option for acquisition donation pages will achieve a higher conversion rate because the express checkout creates friction on the page . because  the express checkout creates friction on the page .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/10/2023 - 03/20/2023
Focus on the Family (FOTF) is a global Christian ministry that receives tons of traffic through its acquisition video series. In the past month, we noticed that the platform that they are using for donations recently rolled out an update that included “express giving options”. We wanted to test the impact this new feature has on their donor base and prospective donors who have not given to FOTF before.
We decided to run an A/B test showing half of the people who hit the landing page the old donation form version without the express giving option, and another half of the people who hit the donation pages the new form that contains the express giving option.
We hypothesized that the express giving option could cause friction in the donation process due to the lack of clarification on the form as to what these express options were and what benefit or value they provided the donor. While options like Google Pay (visible to any google device) and Apple Pay (visible to any Apple device) are more widely known to donors, Anedot pay is a newer feature specific to just Anedot donation pages and could add mental friction for simply being an unknown payment option.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The test showed that removing the express giving option resulted in a significant increase in donations, revenue, and conversion rate.
We do not believe this indicates that express giving options themselves are the problem but rather the options used and the way they are presented. Simply saying "express checkout" on the form leaves the user with more questions than answers as to how these options benefit them. Moreover, Anedot pay is likely too unknown to provide value to the user.
That is why we want to test a version of the form that presents just Apple Pay and Google Pay with a value driven headline. Perhaps, "Save time filling out this form. Give quickly and securely with just 1-click using one of our express giving options."
We will test this next.

experiments.title: How a Wikipedia-style ask performs against a banner ad and a slide-in appeal on article pages during a high-urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a Wikipedia-style banner on article pages will increase recurring donations during a high-urgency campaign because it presents a more compelling appeal in a prevalent location, leveraging urgency and reciprocity to inspire people to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/02/2023 - 03/16/2023
During past high-urgency campaigns, an organization we work with has traditionally run a banner appeal on article pages in an attempt to increase giving from traffic who visit these articles.
Because this organization earns a high volume of traffic to the article pages, we wanted to test the banner appeal against two treatments that we have seen success with in the past to test how they would perform against one another in the context of a high-urgency campaign specifically soliciting recurring donations.
We felt that the banner appeal, while visually attractive and prevalent on the page, lacked a value proposition that was compelling enough to inspire people to make the commitment to give a monthly recurring gift. Both treatments employed nearly identical language both speaking to the problem/solution being conveyed by the appeal and the urgency created by the fact that only 6% of all donors give on a monthly.
The two tactics we wanted to test were a) a Wikipedia-inspired top-of-page appeal that converts to a slide-up appeal when the user scrolls and b) a slide-in appeal that appears from the right side of the screen at about 30% page depth.
For this experiment, we split traffic between the banner appeal (control), the Wikipedia-inspired appeal (treatment A), and the slide-in appeal (treatment B), splitting traffic roughly evenly between each. It was our hypothesis that given its visibility and the appeal it communicates, the Wikipedia inspired ask would perform best.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for two weeks, we were able to validate a 156.9% increase in recurring donations produced by the Wikipedia-inspired banner appeal (99.8% level of confidence). In addition to this encouraging lift in recurring donations, we also observed:
582.2% in desktop recurring donations (34 vs. 5), 99.9% LoC75.5% in mobile recurring donations (14 vs. 8), 80% LoC28.1% in total donations, 89.5% LoC
These results suggest that the Wikipedia-inspired appeal is most effective in engaging visitors and compelling them to donate on a recurring basis.
We believe that the more compelling and thorough language used in the Wikipedia-inspired appeal—detailing the urgency of the problem, conveying the solution the donor can help achieve, and leveraging the persuasion principle of consistency by conveying that only 6% of donors give monthly—led to the increase in recurring gifts as compared to the control (banner).
And we believe that the more prominent location of the Wikipedia-inspired appeal led to the increase in recurring gifts as compared to treatment B (slide-in).
This experiment further validates the effectiveness of the Wikipedia-style banner tactic. Now, future experimentation can broaden this learning by investigating which persuasion principles work best in this context to improve on these results.

experiments.title: How adding impact language to a direct-ask pop up banner during a high urgency campaign affects revenue – marriages saved V1
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the language on the gift array of a direct ask pop-up to show the impact of the gift will result in greater revenue
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/23/2022 - 12/02/2022
Focus on the Family (FOTF) is a global Christian ministry dedicated to helping families thrive. They provide help and resources for couples to build healthy marriages that reflect God’s design and for parents to raise their children according to morals and values grounded in biblical principles. Every year during the calendar year-end high-urgency campaign, FOTF places a top-of-page pop-up banner that contains a direct ask to support their mission. This banner (known internally as the “wiki-banner”) has been tested into over many years, with experiments to improve donation metrics. Since many people visit FOTF.com during this season to make a year-end gift, the “wiki-banner” serves as a low-friction tactic to get people to the donation page quickly. It is important to note that the visiting audience to FOTF’s website is served different content / experiences based on pages they have consumed in the past…specifically a “marriage audience” and an “in favor of pro-life audience – Santicity of Human Life” (also known as SOHL).
For this specific experiment targeting the “marriage audience”, we were focused on changing the language on the gift array buttons to reflect the impact that a gift would make on the amount of marriages saved. The theory was that if we could directly show how many marriages could be saved with a specific gift amount, the visitor would be moved to give a bigger gift to make a greater impact. As screen real-estate is at a premium for this “wiki-banner”, we designed an experience that showed the number of marriages saved on a button on the wiki-banner, and in a “hover-state” it would flip to the equivalent dollar amount of the gift. We even added a free-form field that would allow you to add how many marriages you wanted to save, and it would calculate that amount in real-time.  After making a selection, the user is directed to the donation form where the amount they chose is auto-populated on the giving form, to reduce friction. It is also important to note that we were testing for revenue lift on desktop only (not mobile due to screen size limitations).
experiments.keyLearnings:
If you notice, this experiment is labeled V1. We launched this pre-Giving Tuesday (11/23/22)....and were seeing very positive results...that is until the experiment was "over-ridden" inadvertently by FOTF due to some internal development directives. The over-ride happened right before Giving Tuesday, so we stopped getting results and ended up manually stopping the experiment on Dec 2, 2022. Prior to that stoppage, we recorded 236% lift in revenue for the treatment version with a 99.2% Level of Confidence. By statistical measures, this was a valid lift. Even when we removed outlier donations over $2k from both the control and the treatment, we still saw a significant lift from the treatment.
Due to the technical over-ride, we did not feel comfortable relying on these results to make any future recommendations other than to re-build the experiment as it was, and quickly re-launch to see if the results continued to show a positive lift in revenue through the remainder of the year-end campaign. Results from V2 will be shown in a future write-up and linked here.

experiments.title: How adding impact language to a direct-ask pop up banner during a high urgency campaign affects revenue – babies saved – V3 relaunch
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the language on the gift array of a direct ask pop-up to show the impact of the gift will result in greater revenue
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/11/2022 - 12/31/2022
Focus on the Family (FOTF) is a global Christian ministry dedicated to helping families thrive. They provide help and resources for couples to build healthy marriages that reflect God’s design and for parents to raise their children according to morals and values grounded in biblical principles. Every year during the calendar year-end high-urgency campaign, FOTF places a top-of-page pop-up banner that contains a direct ask to support their mission. This banner (known internally as the “wiki-banner”) has been tested into over many years, with experiments to improve donation metrics. Since many people visit FOTF.com during this season to make a year-end gift, the “wiki-banner” serves as a low-friction tactic to get people to the donation page quickly. It is important to note that the visiting audience to FOTF’s website is served different content / experiences based on pages they have consumed in the past…specifically a “marriage audience” and an “in favor of pro-life audience – Santicity of Human Life” (also known as SOHL).
For this specific experiment targeting the “SOHL“, we were focused on changing the language on the gift array buttons to reflect the impact that a gift would make on the amount of babies saved. The theory was that if we could directly show how many babies could be saved with a specific gift amount, the visitor would be moved to give a bigger gift to make a greater impact. As screen real-estate is at a premium for this “wiki-banner”, we designed an experience that showed the number of babies saved on a button on the wiki-banner, and in a “hover-state” it would flip to the equivalent dollar amount of the gift. We even added a free-form field that would allow you to add how many babies you wanted to save, and it would calculate that amount in real-time.  After making a selection, the user is directed to the donation form where the amount they chose is auto-populated on the giving form, to reduce friction. It is also important to note that we were testing for revenue lift on desktop only (not mobile due to screen size limitations).
NOTE: This is the third version of the experiment (V3 relaunch) due to technical challenges of the V1 + V2 version not properly returning data.
experiments.keyLearnings:
If you notice, this experiment is labeled V3 relaunch. We launched this one AFTER V1 + V2 did not return any data. As it turns out, the previous versions of this experiment did not include a pivotal piece of code that would identify this target audience as having consumed "SOHL" content in the past. As such, the previous experiments returned no data.
Once we corrected the errors, we re-launched this experiment in parallel with the experiment that was targeting "marriage audiences". Like it's "sister-experiment", this version ran from 12/11 to 12/31. Unlike the "sister-version" of this experiment, the results showed an increase in the revenue by around 82% with a 98.6% Level of Confidence....and thus statistically validating for the treatment.
Like its' sister experiment....This led us to even more questions like...
What would results have looked like earlier in the campaign if the code had been correct?Why does this audience respond more positively to impact language than the marriage audience? Does the timing of the experiment change the results?
There were several key take-aways that came from this series of experiments.
Timing/motivation may play a big role in the effectiveness of impact language, but we cannot be sure due to the technical challenges experienced early in the campaign.Make sure you are always monitoring your experiments for data anomalies, and don't be afraid to end an experiment early and re-launch if the data does not "look right"We cannot say definitively that the impact language led to great revenue during the majority of the high-urgency campaign...but we can say that there is enough data to show a positive trend and learnings that we will take into our next high-urgency campaign experiment

experiments.title: How “locking” the donation form on mobile devices impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will locking the donation form on a person’s mobile device screen impact donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/04/2021 - 10/30/2021
Focus on the Family continues to test into new methods to optimize donor conversion on their primary donation form. One of their main objectives is to increase mobile donor conversion. In the past, they had tested into using a two step form on mobile devices that saw a directional lift of 22%. While that test didn’t validate, it did give us insight into the giving experience and impact a tactic like that could have. To continue to test into this idea, but see if we could pull the lever on something that might increase conversion even more and validate the results, we tested into breaking the donation process into steps AND “locking” the donation form on the mobile screen once a person scrolled to the form. At that point, a user would either have to exit out of the donation form or make a gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The multi-step locked screen donation process increased donor conversion significantly by 36%! The locked screen captured the users attention and required them to take an action - either to leave or to donate. Because of this, more people decided to give because there were only two options for them to decide between when given the opportunity to just scroll the donation page, exit, or give. This increased the conversion rate on mobile devices to over 9%!

Here are other notable changes due to the experiment:
New visitors had a 44.9% increase in donations with a 97% level of confidence.
 	Organic Search visitors had a 25.0% increase in donations with a 95% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How removing transactional language on a free trial offer impacted ad clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing transactional language for Facebook ad viewers will achieve an increase in website visits.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/05/2021
When reviewing ads for a free trial offer, we wondered if the language “no credit card needed” was a motivating factor for people to sign up or if it was distancing potential visitors by creating a more transactional mindset and reducing the appeal and overall value of the offer. We wanted to test removing the “no credit card needed” language to see how it impacted clicks on the ad.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we thought that removing transactional language would come across as a more humanized approach and personal in nature, what we found was that removing the "no credit card needed" language caused a 25.9% decrease in traffic with a 99.9% level of confidence. What this data shows us is this language actually decreases anxiety and removes friction from the process, making it a lower perceived amount of effort to accept the offer because people are not worried about having to remember to cancel their free trial at a later date to avoid being charged any membership fees. Because we achieved valid results, we were able to take this learning from a Facebook ad and apply it to other areas and entry points where a free trial is offered on the website and add in this language to increase conversion rates.

experiments.title: How a more conversational tone in a subject line impacts open rate

rows.name: You still have time to double your...

experiments.researchQuestion: Which email would get more people to open it?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/12/2017 - 12/19/2017
While we weren’t able to validate this experiment based off of donations, which is the ultimate goal, we were able to validate the experiment on opens. The treatment subject line took a more conversational approach that didn’t give any reference to giving or donating by saying, “Nothing is impossible for God”. This approach increased opens by 77%! Knowing that we can significantly increase the amount of people who see and read the email with this kind of approach, we recommend testing this approach again and doing a 50/50 split of the email file so that we have a greater chance at validating it off of donations to see what the impact of this approach really has on donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: The emails for Focus on the Family’s year-end giving campaign are image heavy with a video on them to push people to the donation page. We hypothesized that a personal email, with a more conversational tone from the subject line to the body copy would increase conversion. The first goal was to get more people to open the emails in an effort to get more people to click and convert. We A/B split the email file and tested it.

experiments.title: How the gift frequency section on a primary donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the way the gift frequency section was presented and functioned for prospective donors will increase donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/14/2021 - 02/12/2021
Focus on the Family recently changed their donation platform. During that transition, the way the gift frequency section was presented looked and functioned differently than before. They heard from prospective donors that it was slightly confusing for them as they made a gift. To test into the best approach for that section on the page, we developed two different variations in hopes that we could increase clarity to the giving process. The first variation presented the two different gift frequency options as checked boxes – requiring the prospective donor to have to select the gift they wanted to make through the checked boxes. The second variation presented the options as buttons. Again, requiring the prospective donor to have to select the gift they wanted by selecting the specific button. This experiment originally ran during the last few weeks of their calendar year-end campaign and showed a negative impact on donor conversion from both variations. We proposed testing this again outside of year-end, during neutral motivation.
experiments.keyLearnings: Similar to when we tested these variations at year-end, there was a negative impact on donor conversion from both treatments. In fact, the most significant decrease (16%) in donor conversion came from the button variation. The checked boxes also saw a 10% dip in donations as well. This experiment is a difficult one to fully understand because the two variations that were developed were intended to bring clarity to the giving process - something that donors had communicated that they needed. Instead, we added friction to the giving process. We hypothesize that the current control requires no work at all. In fact, We think that donors don't really notice it or understand it so they just continue to progress down the giving form to make their gift. The two new variations may be making the donor pause and think about the action they want to take when they get to that section. Continued testing needs to be done to: 1) gain greater clarity to the motivation of the donor and elements of friction on the page 2) explore our hypothesis about the gift frequency section altogether. To do this, our next test is going to be to completely remove the "Choose your gift frequency" section and present a recurring option in a different way.

experiments.title: How presenting a micro-ask instead of a direct donation ask in a giving widget impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will presenting a softer call-to-action on the giving widget increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/10/2020 - 12/26/2020
During Focus on the Family’s year-end campaign, they feature a giving widget at the top of their navigation. On the left hand side they have a banner with value proposition copy and on the right hand side they feature a mini giving form where people can decide how much they want to give. After a person selects how much they want to give, they continue to the donation page to complete their gift. We questioned if this was the best approach to convert donors. So to test into it, we presented a softer call-to-action in the giving widget. We presented website visitors with a question where they would then decided “yes” or “no”. The question we asked was “Would you consider giving hope to families this Christmas?”. Both “yes” and “no” buttons led to a donation page where they could make a gift. We hypothesized that pushing the decision to give to the donation page might increase donor conversion instead of having people make the decision to give and decide how much they want to give right when they see the widget.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The micro-ask decrease overall donor conversion by 9.5%. To our surprise the direct ask and gift array selection actually improves donor conversion. Our hypothesis behind this is the motivation of traffic coming to the website during this season. There are so many channels pushing people to the Focus website to give. Once they get to the site, they may have already decided to give. Another hypothesis is that the soft micro-ask might be a bit of a whiplash. If I say "yes" I want to give families hope at Christmas and then am taken to a donation page, the experience may be too big of a jump.

Here are some additional observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment - most of them reinforcing our hypothesis around visitor motivation:
Email visitors had a 160.5% decrease in donations with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Paid Search visitors had a 40.2% decrease in donations with an 88% level of confidence.
 	Mobile visitors had a 35.1% decrease in donations with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Returning visitors had a 15.8% decrease in donations with a 99% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How the way an offer is presented impacts name and donor conversion

rows.name: From Anger to Affection - Video Series
From Anger to Affection - Online Course

experiments.researchQuestion: What kind of language is more appealing to people for the content offer?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/15/2019 - 06/05/2019
Focus on the Family has developed video series to use as new name and donor acquisition offers. These offers are promoted through paid Facebook ads that lead people to an acquisition page and an instant donation ask. They have called these offers “video series”. These 4-6 session video series also include key discussion points for each session/lesson and Scripture memory verses to help reinforce the content that people consume and learn.
NextAfter has developed similar offers like these for other organizations, but call them, “online courses”.
In an effort to optimize these offers for Focus on the Family, and learn what is most appealing and motivating to prospective names and donors, we proposed testing the kind of language used to promote and describe them. The control group used “video series” through out the acquisition funnel and the treatment group used “online course” in all of the copy.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we hypothesized that the "online course" would increase donor conversion because an online course has a higher perceived value than a video series, we found that the "online course" language actually decreased donor conversion by 25%. In addition to this, we also saw a 5% decrease in names acquired with the "online course" language. The "online course" version didn't perform as well as the "video series" one because people may have actually been too intimidated by the thought of taking an online course. Calling something a "video series" probably has a higher appeal and feels more accessible to more people than an online course does. This resulted in less people signing up for the online course and even less people converting as donors.

The video series acquisition offers have been a huge success and driver of new names and donors for Focus on the Family. The learnings of this experiment are incredibly valuable for the video series they already have developed and the asserted effort and resources they are putting in to making more video series to use for acquisition in the next year.

This approach is also something we'd like to test with other organizations who are offering "online courses" to see if the results would be consistent.

experiments.title: How inline acquisition offers in place of ads affected clickthrough
experiments.researchQuestion: Will replacing the mid-roll advertising banner with an inline acquisition increase clicks for this placement?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/30/2017 - 11/09/2017
Focus on the Family currently uses DoubleClick for Publishers to manage ad fill throughout their website.  Most of the ads in rotation are for their own resources, giving opportunities, and some third-party offers. One such placement is a mid-roll banner targeting long-form article pages.  They hypothesized that an inline email acquisition offer that is contextually relevant for their audience may increase clickthrough and help capture more of the high volume of new visitors viewing these articles.
They already had a free online Marriage Assessment which has resonated well with visitors to their marriage and parenting content, so they split the traffic to articles in those categories and delivered an inline offer for the assessment in place of the mid-roll ad from DoubleClick to half the audience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The inline treatment increased the number of offer clicks by over 1,870%! This learning shows how the alignment of an offer, as well as its treatment, can be put to work to acquire more names. We hypothesize that a large portion of this impact is due to “banner blindness”—a web usability phenomenon where visitors consciously or subconsciously ignore banner-like information.  Banners also limit the amount of value proposition you can effectively communicate, which can decrease perceived value.
With high traffic and known content themes, we plan to test further into this placement—including moving it to the end of the content where we hypothesize we may be able to capitalize on the natural pivot/decision point created by completing the article.  Other potential tests include the headline, button, and value proposition copy as well as the inclusion of supporting graphical elements.

experiments.title: How removing the call-to-action at the top of a landing page impacts name conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing the design and navigational elements increase the visitors getting to the assessment?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/24/2017 - 10/30/2017
The treatment decreased the amount of people getting to the assessment by 28.6%! While we hypothesized that the simplified page would help decrease friction and motivate more people to click-through because the focus of the page was on the value proposition, we learned that:
People coming to this page were already highly motivated to take the assessment. The additional value proposition focus wasn’t needed to convert these people.
By removing the call-to-action at the top of the page, we made it harder for these highly motivated people to click-through to the actual assessment.
We recommend building upon these results and learnings and continue testing separate elements on this page to get a better understanding of the motivation of the audience.
experiments.keyLearnings: Focus on the Family has a Marriage Assessment on their website to help couples grow and strengthen their marriage. Before an end-user gets to the actual assessment there is a landing page with value proposition and a call-to-action. In an effort to increase conversion on this page of people getting to the assessment, they developed a treatment. They hypothesized that by simplifying the page – including removing the call-to-action at the top of the page – they might be able to motivate more people to take it by leading with the value proposition of the assessment. The goal of the treatment was to eliminate any elements on the page that would cause potential friction in the conversion process. They split the traffic coming to the assessment page and tested the control against the treatment.

experiments.title: How adding a visual treatment to a tabbed donation form during a non-urgent campaign season affects donor conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a visual treatment to a monthly giving tab on the main donation page  for visitors to a general donation page will achieve a greater number of recurring gift givers.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/01/2022 - 08/21/2022
Focus on the Family is a global Christian ministry dedicated to helping families thrive. They provide help and resources for couples to build healthy marriages that reflect God’s design, and for parents to raise their children according to morals and values grounded in biblical principles.
While they have a main donation page that is always reachable from the main page, they often run high urgency campaigns that can sometimes skew results for general donations. For the time period of this test, Focus was not planning on having any high urgency campaigns running, so we felt it was a good time to test some things on the main donation page, without the potential results skewing that can sometimes be attributed to high urgency campaigns, where motivations to give can be elevated.
The main donation page has a two-tabbed design that separates a one-time gift from a monthly donation. We wanted to test our hypothesis that by visually drawing more attention to a monthly donation tab on a general donation page, you could increase overall donations (and specifically for recurring giving), even during a period of non-urgency.
For this test, we placed an animated heart that floated above the monthly tab that drew attention to the monthly gift option. The goal of the test was to see if this treatment could get a lift in monthly recurring gifts.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While there was potentially a novelty effect from this experiment, it was very interesting that the treatment for this test validated with a 97.7% level of confidence giving the treatment a 19.3% increase in the total number of donations for all traffic. Again, this experiment ran during a period where there were no urgent campaign factors that could have potentially skewed the results. As for the effect on recurring donations, the treatment experienced a 99.4% increase with a 92% level of confidence.
Of particular note, the experiment received a much larger lift in donations from the desktop, with a 25% lift and a 100% level of confidence. For recurring donations on the desktop, we saw a massive 141% lift in recurring donations with a 94.4% level of confidence (0.1% shy of validating).
On mobile, the inverse was true. We saw an 11% decrease in donations w/ a 50% LoC, and recurring donations on mobile were completely flat, with no change
Key takeaways: Drawing attention to the recurring donation tab on a desktop setting had a positive lift and is worth iterating on to see if we can get an additional lift with variations of the animation effect...seasonality changes, color, etc. As for mobile, we should experiment with more subtle ways to get the same desired effect as the desktop. It was clear that the mobile experience was not working in it's current state, and could use some additional help.

experiments.title: How a sticky donate button with a campaign specific CTA effects donor conversion during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a CTA on a sticky donate button related to an ongoing high urgency campaign will result in a higher number of donations, than a generic donate button.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/07/2022 - 06/22/2022
Focus on the Family runs a high urgency campaign every year centered around pro-life issues. This year, they wanted to test if adding a sticky donate button to article pages with a call to action mirroring the ongoing high urgency campaign would result in higher donor conversions than a similar button with a generic call to action. It is worth noting that this experiment was limited to a segmented audience that used user data to identify if a user had prior pro-life interests. For the control, a generic donate button (with a pulsing heart icon) was placed on article pages that matched the data segmentation. For the treatment, a sticky button with the words ” Save a Baby” (with a pulsing baby icon) was placed on article pages that matched the data segmentation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
During this experiment that had 91,000 samples over a two week period, we saw a 55% increase for the treatment with a 95.5% Level of Confidence. In this case, this experiment validated for the hypothesis. Key takeaways from this experiment are that matching the CTA of the button to the segmented audience during a high urgency campaign can result in a greater conversion.
Next steps for the experiment would be to test his same hypothesis using the same data segments but do so during a non-high urgency campaign to see if properly segmented users are still converted at a higher frequency with a specific CTA.

experiments.title: How creating a security-themed visual enhancement to a secure donation form affects donor conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a visual treatment to the donation form that appears more secure for potential donors entering their credit card info will achieve a greater completion rate and a higher number of donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/20/2022 - 06/18/2022
Focus on the Family uses a donation platform that has a fairly standard contact and payment form. The current platform provider uses a form that asks for name and credit card info and is also located on a secure server, but we felt like the form could use some visual enhancement around security features. While the native form was indeed secure, we felt like creating some visual call-outs around the area where you enter your credit card info might actually lead to more completed transactions and thus, more donations. The treatment for this experiment placed a grey box around the credit card info along with a secure lock icon. The control remained the standard layout of a white box with no lock icon. We ran this test on a single content offer that was being promoted on Facebook, so that we could have enough traffic to potentially validate the results.
experiments.keyLearnings: As hypothesized, the treatment with greater perceived security did create a sizeable lift in transaction completitions and total donations. We saw a massive 126% lift in total donations with a 97.8% level of confidence. The perceived increase in security had the effect we were looking for - an increase in form completions and a greater number of donations, but it also was an experiment that had very little potential downside. With this big of a lift, we felt the improvements were drastic enough to roll out the treatment as a standardization across all 1500+ donation forms on Focus on the Family. We started by creating a personalization through Google Optimize that would apply the treatment to all donation forms. Once we saw that the treatment did not have any adverse effects for other known offers with donation forms, we worked with the team at Anedot (the donation platform provider) to open up the source code to allow us to hard code this as a standardized feature for all forms. This has now been implemented as the control going forward. This is a continuation of multiple tactical tests that we completed to improve the performance of a standard donation form for Focus on the Family. Future variants of this test could be testing if background color variations improve conversions or if additional callout text increases form fill completion rates.

experiments.title: How adding more appeal to the value proposition on a donation page affects donor conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more appeal to the value proposition for readers of an instant donation page will achieve a higher number of donor conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/25/2022 - 05/27/2022
Focus on the Family has a number of free video series on topics around Faith, Marriage, and Parenting. The goal of putting out these free video series is to share free content around these topics and to collect names and email addresses of interested subscribers and then eventually nurture them towards a donation to the mission. The process is to promote the free video series on social media campaign, drive traffic to a landing page where they collect info like email address and name to send a link to access the free series, then upon a successful info collection, immediately send the subscriber to an instant donation page where they have the opportunity to provide a gift.
As we evaluate opportunities for optimization all along the marketing funnel, we look for metrics that show areas where we can make improvements in traffic conversion (CPMs, CTRs, CPCs), email conversions (Signup conversion rates), and instant donation conversions (ICRs). For this particular experiment, this instant donation page is part of the overall campaign strategy and what we would refer to as the “bottom of the marketing funnel”. The donor conversion rates on this campaign were lower than average, so we felt there was an opportunity to make improvements.
We decided to test if adding more appeal into the body copy would result in a higher number of donor conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment validated for the control with a 44% decrease in donor conversions with a 97/7% level of confidence.
This experiment used one of the 4 tenets of a well-executed value proposition - appeal. In hindsight, we probably should have applied more of the other elements, like exclusivity and clarity. While we did reduce the number of paragraphs, the overall body copy grew, and could have probably been more clear. For the future, we will attempt to add more of the other elements.

experiments.title: How adding an impact statement under the donate button affects conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an impact statement under the donate button for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/03/2022 - 03/10/2022
In a recent experiment, we hypothesized that adding an impact statement directly under the donate button would decrease abandonment and thus increase conversion rates. While the treatment in that experience did produce an 11.9% lift in conversion rates, our sample size prevented us from validating our experiment.
We decided to run a similar experiment in an effort to validate our hypothesis and to improve flat conversion rates on a donation page where visitors land after signing up for one of Focus on the Family’s video series. As in our previous experiment, we developed a treatment that included an impact statement directly below the donate button. And because we wanted to validate the lift we saw before, we left the messaging untouched. We again hypothesized that this would help decrease abandonment by highlighting the value of their donation. For this experiment, we ran an A/B test with traffic split 50/50.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 64 days, we observed a 24.4% lift in donor conversion rates with a 97% level of confidence, validating our hypothesis that adding an impact statement under the donate button would improve conversion rates.
We believe that this lift resulted in part from using impact language that showed what specifically has been accomplished when donors give. In addition to adding value, this treatment also shifts the perspective from "we" (organizational) language to donor-centric language that puts them at the heart of the solution.
We also believe that placing the impact statement near (in this case directly under) the donation button may have reduced abandonment as it provides one more reminder of the tangible value of the donor's contribution.
Interestingly, returning visitors had an 89.5% increase in donations, indicating that the impact statement may have inspired donors who previously reached the donation page but did not give to donate this time around.
This experiment validated (and even improved upon) the lift of our prior experiment and reinforced the value of showing donors the impact of their donations. Based on these findings, we believe that such an impact statement should be included on all of Focus on the Family's donation pages. It may also be worth testing the impact of moving the impact statement above the donation form so that donors see it earlier in the transaction.

Returning visitors had an 89.5% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidenceEmail visitors had a 216.1% increase in donations with an 90% level of confidence

experiments.title: How a sticky bar with a “special message” on a donation page impacted gifts from returning visitors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a personalized sticky bar for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/07/2022 - 03/21/2022
In an effort to improve donor conversion, we wanted to further “humanize” the instant donation page for Focus on the Family’s video series subscribers. We developed a treatment that included a special message sticky bar from a Vice President encouraging visitors to remain on the page and take the time read the donation page offer. The sticky bar included a photo of the VP and the following note: “Thank you for signing up for this video series! Before you go, please take a moment to read the important message below to learn how you can help more families get access to free video series like this one.” We split traffic 50/50 and launched this treatment on several active donation pages to observe the impact of the sticky bar on donation conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the experiment slightly over one week, we saw a 33% lift in donor conversion rate with 95% level of confidence. We were especially interested in how returning visitors would respond to this message since it was a new highlight to previous donation pages they would have experienced. For that segment specifically we saw an 83% increase in donations with a 96% level of confidence. We will continue to run the sticky bar to 100% of traffic based on these results but we will also move forward with testing special messages from other staff members at Focus to see if we can continue to improve and optimize the treatment by continually rotating how the page appears for repeat visitors to hold their attention.

experiments.title: How removing the ways to give and additional donation information section impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing the additional ways to giving and donation information from the bottom of the page increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/11/2021 - 07/11/2021
As Focus on the Family continues to optimize their primary donation page since moving to a new platform six months ago, they are testing into every element on the page. This even includes elements on the page that are below the actual giving form. When looking at the donation page, they wondered if the section under the giving form that communicated all of the different ways a person could give and had additional legal information (not necessary to include on the page in full text)  about a person’s donation was impacting donor conversion. They tested this by simply removing both of those sections on the page.

Removing the additional ways to give and the donation disclosure information, donor conversion decreased by 26%. experiments.title: How presenting the gift frequency as a tabbed form impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: What is the impact a tabbed giving form will have on conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/25/2021 - 04/25/2021
As Focus on the Family continued on their quest to optimize the way the gift frequency section was presented, they wanted to test into a tabbed form. This tabbed form would show the gift frequency options as their own form and presented as the first option a donor had to select on the giving form. This changed the order of the donation pathway. Since we recently tested into having the gift frequency as a checkbox, this variation was the control. We also developed two different treatment variations – one that included just the tabbed form and another that included the tabbed form AND the steps to make a donation.
experiments.keyLearnings: The tabbed form with the steps increased donor conversion by 15%. Interestingly, the tabbed form without the steps didn't have a positive or negative impact on conversion. Because the steps were included in the control, this tells us that the tabbed form by itself isn't the element that causes the increase in conversion, but when you pair the tabbed form WITH the steps it does. People still need the clarity provided in the steps in order to make their gift. The tabbed form pushes the gift frequency/type to the top of the donation pathway. This reduces friction in the giving process as a person has momentum in moving down the form.

experiments.title: How combining offers into one campaign in Facebook affects donor acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Reducing competition in our own campaigns will increase efficiency
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/08/2020 - 09/07/2020
The Focus acquisition team was running multiple different video series offers on Facebook, which meant that those budgets were competing with each other in the Facebook market. For instance the Raising Kids to Defend Your Faith video series was competing against the Hope for Your Hurting Marriage series, because they were targeting the same audience but different budgets to acquire within those audiences. We wanted to test combining those offers into one campaign to see if that increase efficiency in donor acquisition. So we did a split test, testing four single video series offer campaigns against one campaign with ads for all four of the video offers in it.
experiments.keyLearnings: This test resulted in a 43% increase in donor conversion, meaning that Facebook had to show the ads to fewer people in order to get them to sign up and donate. That resulted in a 9% difference in cost per donor, cutting it down from a $22.64 cost per donor to a $20.42 cost per donor.

experiments.keyLearnings: The video treatment resulted in much higher engagement overall. It increased the click through rate by 124% and the comments by 100%. Facebook used this creative much more often because of the higher results. The video has a 34% lower cost per click, taking the cost from .67 to .44. There was an increase in donation as well because of the increase in traffic, but the proportions of the samples was so vast that the test was invalid.

experiments.title: How presenting a micro-ask instead of a direct donation ask in a giving widget impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will presenting a softer call-to-action on the giving widget increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/10/2020 - 12/26/2020
During Focus on the Family’s year-end campaign, they feature a giving widget at the top of their navigation. On the left hand side they have a banner with value proposition copy and on the right hand side they feature a mini giving form where people can decide how much they want to give. After a person selects how much they want to give, they continue to the donation page to complete their gift. We questioned if this was the best approach to convert donors. So to test into it, we presented a softer call-to-action in the giving widget. We presented website visitors with a question where they would then decided “yes” or “no”. The question we asked was “Would you consider giving hope to families this Christmas?”. Both “yes” and “no” buttons led to a donation page where they could make a gift. We hypothesized that pushing the decision to give to the donation page might increase donor conversion instead of having people make the decision to give and decide how much they want to give right when they see the widget.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The micro-ask decrease overall donor conversion by 9.5%. To our surprise the direct ask and gift array selection actually improves donor conversion. Our hypothesis behind this is the motivation of traffic coming to the website during this season. There are so many channels pushing people to the Focus website to give. Once they get to the site, they may have already decided to give. Another hypothesis is that the soft micro-ask might be a bit of a whiplash. If I say "yes" I want to give families hope at Christmas and then am taken to a donation page, the experience may be too big of a jump.

Here are some additional observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment - most of them reinforcing our hypothesis around visitor motivation:
Email visitors had a 160.5% decrease in donations with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Paid Search visitors had a 40.2% decrease in donations with an 88% level of confidence.
 	Mobile visitors had a 35.1% decrease in donations with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Returning visitors had a 15.8% decrease in donations with a 99% level of confidence.

Since transitioning to a new donation platform, over the past few months Focus on the Family has been testing into the best approach to present the gift frequency section. The checkbox is simple. It makes sense to people. There is no question about what to do or how to make a gift or make it recurring. This increased clarity led to more people opting in to recurring giving and creating an easier and better giving experience for all donors.
