--Moving forward, we will work this sense of urgency into our copy as it specifically relates to the video series topic at hand as it seems to be a huge motivator for our traffic that is already familiar with the ministry.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 38.9% increase with a 93% level of confidence.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Tablet visitors had a 5.2% increase in donations with a 98% level of confidence.Referral visitors had a 677.8% increase in donations with a 99% level of confidence.(Other) visitors had a 7.0% increase in donations with a 93% level of confidence.Male visitors had a 151.8% increase in donations with an 88% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How adding social proof impacted email acquisition on a landing page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding social proof for landing page visitors will achieve greater email conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/18/2021 - 02/10/2022
Focus on the Family reached a milestone of over 1 million sign-ups for their free video series, and we wanted to test our hypothesis that highlighting this as social proof on the landing page would increase email sign-ups on the landing page. We added a call out underneath the image to “Join over 1 million people who have been reached with encouraging, faith-building content across all our free video series like this one!”
experiments.keyLearnings:
We saw a directional lift of 4% on the treatment page with an 89.9% level of confidence. Highlighting the number of people who have accessed the free video series seemed to compel landing page visitors to move forward with completing the registration form. This treatment specifically worked well for mobile visitors and email visitors. Based on these promising results, we will look for additional opportunities to test adding social proof elements like this one on future offers.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
(Other) visitors had a 4.1% decrease in emails acquired with a 91% level of confidence.
 	Mobile visitors had a 5.2% increase in emails acquired with a 92% level of confidence.
 	Female visitors had a 10.0% decrease in emails acquired with a 98% level of confidence.
 	65+ visitors had a 19.0% increase in emails acquired with a 91% level of confidence.
 	Male visitors had a 37.0% increase in emails acquired with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Organic Search visitors had a 100.0% decrease in emails acquired with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Direct visitors had a 100.0% decrease in emails acquired with a 100% level of confidence.
 	35-44 visitors had a 40.0% increase in emails acquired with a 99% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 91.7% increase in emails acquired with a 100% level of confidence.
 	25-34 visitors had a 54.5% decrease in emails acquired with a 100% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How simplifying the primary header on the Focus site for mobile devices impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase donor conversion by removing additional clutter and creating clarity to the navigation header?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/25/2021 - 09/10/2021
Focus on the Family was running a high urgency giving campaign called SeeLife. During the campaign they featured a donation wikibanner at the top of the site’s primary header and navigation. When looking at the experience on mobile devices, there was a lot of clutter with the addition of this wikibanner to what was already on the site. We hypothesized this was causing friction to the donation pathway. To test into this, we developed two variations. For one of them, we removed the CTA’s in the header that were not needed to make a gift, but kept the “donate” button. For the second version, we removed the CTA’s in the header that were not needed to make a gift, removed the donate button, but added a line of copy above the gift array that said, “please select the amount you would like to give”.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we weren't able to validate the experiment, but we did see get a directional lift with one variation and got even greater clarity with the user experience.
We are learning that donor's are experiencing the most friction with the wikibanner itself. When we added the call to action on top of the gift array, we were able to increase donor conversion (directionally) by 11%! Revenue experienced a 15.4% increase with a 92% level of confidence.
Just adding the donate button back in to the experience had no impact on donor conversion.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Paid Search visitors had a 113.8% increase in donations with a 99% level of confidence.New  visitors had a 12.5% increase in donations with an 89% level of confidence.35-44 visitors had a 127.1% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.45-54 visitors had an 84.4% increase in donations with a 98% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How the focusing on the fact that a content offer is free impacts donation conversion on the acquisition donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: Focusing on the fact that an offer is free may decrease donation conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/19/2021 - 04/12/2021
As Focus on the Family increased their acquisition efforts, complaints that the donation page was misleading increased as well. People expressed concern that the donation page language made it seem as though they had to donate to receive the offer. So we tested their control copy with a focus group and only 61% of the people who read the page said it was clear that the video series was free. Our next step was to include the word free when the video series was mentioned on the donation page, in the body copy and in the header. We tested that with a new focus group and 70% of them said it was clear that the video series was free. So it was time to test it in the market place to see if this new donation page language would increase or decrease donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The emphasis on free throughout the copy on this page decreased donor conversion by 21% at a directional level of confidence. This held true across all devices and demographics.

Our hypothesis is that the consistent mention of the series being free may have confused the reader around the purpose of the donation page. It may have also made it easier for them not to consider a donation. Or the control may have just made people feel like they needed to give.

Our next step is to test more straight forward language. This test included the word free in many places, but it never addressed the donation ask head on. We recommend testing language that is very clear in the purpose behind the ask such as "We want to be honest with you. You don't have to give to receive the video series. It's in your email inbox now. But it's only there because people have given to Focus on the Family so that you can have it. If you think this ministry is important to share with others, will you join our donors in reaching more people who really, truly need this video series?"

experiments.title: How a lower gift array affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: A lower gift array will raise donor conversion on an acquisition page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/27/2021 - 05/27/2021
Focus on the Family had a variety of gift arrays on their acquisition donation pages. We wanted to see which array worked best for acquisition, especially since people new to Focus would be more likely to give if the ask was not too steep for a first time donor.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Overall, most of the segments saw a directional or a valid lift in donor conversion with the lower gift array. And the lower gift array did not decrease conversion in any segments with large enough samples or any meaningful level of confidence. For instance mobile saw a 17% increase at an 88% level of confidence, while desktop saw only a 2% increase at a 13% level of confidence. All indications are that the lower gift array helps and it does not hurt any large segment.

Our hypothesis is that starting at $25 rather than $50 gives a mental ladder up to the $50 gift for the premium, rather than just starting at $50. So more people would give because a $25 gift would be easier donation for most of the page visitors to give. We expected this to be especially true to new visitors who were not as familiar with Focus on the Family and would be turned off at such a high initial donation ask. But the data suggest that primarily returning visitors saw this page and that they were also more likely to give if the array started off in a smaller dollar ask.

And, the best news is that this lower gift array did not lead to a decrease in revenue. There was a 2.3% increase in revenue per visitor at a 53% level of confidence. This tells us that with more people donating, the slight decrease in average gift (from $53 to $48) did no harm to the overall revenue. So we will be rolling this gift array out to all acquisition pages and recommend it is rolled out to Housefile donation pages as well.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Referral visitors had a 33.8% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Mobile visitors had a 17.5% increase in donations with an 89% level of confidence.
 	Tablet visitors had a 150.8% increase in donations with an 87% level of confidence.
 	Returning visitors had a 12.4% increase in donations with an 86% level of confidence.
 	Organic Search visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Female visitors had a 38.4% increase in donations with a 94% level of confidence.
 	45-54 visitors had a 3.1% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.
 	25-34 visitors had a 10.4% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a two-step donation form impacts donor conversion on mobile devices during year-end
experiments.researchQuestion:
Will a two-step donation form increase donor conversion on mobile devices during a high-urgency campaign?
The two-step form saw a directional lift of 22%! While we weren’t able to validate the results, we were able to see a positive increase in conversion rate! This positive directional lift is mostly due to the high volume of email traffic coming to the donation form. People’s motivation is slightly higher from this traffic source and the decrease in perceived friction to the giving experience helped to convert more of these people. We would like to test this again during non high-urgency campaigns. We would also like to test into the order of the information we are asking for from people. Take a look at other interesting data points from specific segments:
New visitors had a 19.4% increase in donations with an 87% level of confidence.
Email visitors had a 42.3% increase in donations with a 97% level of confidence.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/14/2020 - 12/26/2020
During Focus on the Family’s year-end campaign, we wanted to see if we could improve donor conversion on mobile devices. Mobile donor conversion has been something that has been difficult to impact in the past during high-urgency campaigns. So to continue to test into this, we developed a more radical giving experience by splitting the donation form into two steps. We hypothesized that it might be easier and a better user experience to break the form into separate steps rather than showing people the full form all at once. We hypothesized that we might be able to decrease the perceived cost of the number of form fields on the page with the two-step approach.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The two-step form saw a directional lift of 22%! While we weren't able to validate the results, we were able to see a positive increase in conversion rate! This positive directional lift is mostly due to the high volume of email traffic coming to the donation form. People's motivation is slightly higher from this traffic source and the decrease in perceived friction to the giving experience helped to convert more of these people. We would like to test this again during non high-urgency campaigns. We would also like to test into the order of the information we are asking for from people. Take a look at other interesting data points from specific segments:
New visitors had a 19.4% increase in donations with an 87% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 42.3% increase in donations with a 97% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How the way the gift frequency section on the the primary donation page is presented impacts conversion during year-end
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the way the gift frequency section was presented and functioned for prospective donors during year-end will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/23/2020 - 01/08/2021
Focus on the Family recently changed their donation platform. During that transition, the way the gift frequency section was presented looked and functioned differently than before. They heard from prospective donors that it was slightly confusing for them as they made a gift. To test into the best approach for that section on the page, we developed two different variations in hopes that we could increase clarity to the giving process. The first variation presented the two different gift frequency options as checked boxes – requiring the prospective donor to have to select the gift they wanted to make through the checked boxes. The second variation presented the options as buttons. Again, requiring the prospective donor to have to select the gift they wanted by selecting the specific button. This experiment was run during the last few weeks of their calendar year-end campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the experiment didn't validate, we did see a directional lift in donor conversion from the checked boxes variation, and no difference in conversion from the button variation.

One of the big questions around this experiment was the impact the variations had on recurring giving . We hypothesized that the control variation may see an increase in recurring because of the lack of clarity it presented to donors about how they could make a one-time gift. In analyzing that data, we found that the control did see a significant increase in recurring donor conversion. In fact, the checked box variation decreased recurring transactions by 41% and the button variation decreased recurring transactions by 33% (88% valid). That said, the organization is seeing an increase in recurring gift cancellations right now. We hypothesize that this is because of the confusion that the control may have been causing in the kind of gift a donor was making.

Additionally, this experiment was run during a time of year when motivation of donors is at its highest. Because of this and the impact we saw around recurring gifts, we are retesting this approach this month so that we can have a larger data sample and more neutral motivation.

experiments.title: How adding a pulsing star icon to a sticky bar impacted clicks

rows.name: Sticky Bar Free Trial w/ Testimonial
Sticky Bar Free Trial w/ Star& Pulsing Button

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a pulsing icon for website visitors will achieve increased clicks on the sticky bar.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/11/2020 - 10/12/2020
As we looked to increase clicks on a sticky bar to begin a free trial, we developed a treatment that added a pulsing star icon to the sticky bar. All other elements remained the same. We wanted to emphasize this as the primary call to action for website visitors since there are multiple buttons on the page with additional calls to action. We hypothesized that adding an animated element to this specific button would help it stand out more on the page as the preferred call to action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
When looking across all traffic, we did not validate results on the experiment. However, we did see that email visitors had a 272.3% increase in begin free trial signup with a 97% level of confidence, while all other segments indicated a directional decrease. This could be a result of the consistency between the messaging communicated via email and the corresponding offer on the page. We'll continue to do additional testing to introduce other elements to drive free trial signups for additional segments.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Paid Search visitors had a 16.4% decrease in begin free trial signup with a 91% level of confidence.
 	All Traffic visitors had a 9.9% decrease in begin free trial signup with a 93% level of confidence.
 	Desktop visitors had a 14.7% decrease in begin free trial signup with a 92% level of confidence.
 	Tablet visitors had a 24.4% decrease in begin free trial signup with an 88% level of confidence.
 	Returning visitors had a 13.1% decrease in begin free trial signup with a 94% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How the homepage callout influences recurring donations

rows.name: One Button w/ New Value Prop

experiments.researchQuestion: Will using the homepage callout to emphasize recurring giving increase the number of recurring givers acquired?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/17/2018 - 01/26/2018
During the month of January, there was a concerted effort to acquire new monthly donors through the Friends of Focus on the Family program. Included in these efforts was radio programming that pushed listeners to go online to become a recurring donor. To take advantage of this visitor motivation, we wanted to see if focusing the callout on the homepage on recurring giving would help influence the visitors’ likelihood to become a recurring donors. Along with this experiment, we wanted to validate if a value proposition that spent more time laying out the benefits of ongoing support could further increase acquisition.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for 2 weeks, this experiment did not produce a statistically valid result. When evaluating the conversions, we realized that the recurring gift offer may be the wrong offer for this section. While percentages may seem wildly different, the number of recurring gifts made, regardless of the treatment, was not high enough to get close to validating. We also saw that a donor is 6x more likely to click on the one-time gift option in the navigation than they are the recurring gift option. With this finding, it may make sense to repurpose that homepage callout section to focus on individual gifts in the short term. Concurrently, we can test new value propositions on the recurring gift donation page and, once validated, we can retesting the new value proposition on the homepage.

experiments.title: How daily budget optimization performs against lifetime budget optimization in Facebook ads
experiments.researchQuestion: Does daily budget optimization outperform lifetime budget optimization in getting more instant donors at a lower cost?
experiments.keyLearnings: Although the cost to acquire an instant donor was slightly lower with daily budget optimization, we saw a 23% decrease in conversion rate using this feature. Though the experiment did not validate, we hypothesize that using lifetime budget optimization allows Facebook to spend more at certain times of the day/week that users are most active, rather than forcing it to spend the budget each day, will lead to greater scalability and an increase in donor conversion long-term.

experiments.title: How presenting segmented offers based on a person’s engagement on parenting articles impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will offering people targeted premium offers increase donor conversion on parenting articles?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/15/2019 - 03/26/2019
Focus on the Family has done significant testing within the articles on their website. From testing the kind of offer to the placement of the offers, they have optimized and continue to optimize the conversion opportunities on the articles pages for the significant volume of traffic. Most recently they learned that presenting people a premium/book at the end of the articles, that readers could receive by giving a gift of any amount, had the most significant impact on donor conversion. To continue to test into this learning, they hypothesized that presenting people with a targeted offer based on their engagement on the Focus on the Family website might increase donor conversion. Focus identified the top premium offers based on what is most requested and the kind of content that people read and consume from their site and offered premiums that closely aligned with those topics. This approach was tested against offering people one single premium offer.
experiments.keyLearnings:
As we’ve continued to monitor and dig into the results of this experiment, we have discovered an interesting finding that is misrepresenting the results and learnings. A significant portion of the traffic on the article pages are either first time visitors to the Focus website, or this is the first article they’ve consumed from Focus. This audience has not been receiving targeted content offers based on cookies. Because of this, 68% of the donations from the treatment results have been receiving the single content offer/premium.
After discovering this and learning what was happening, we have paused the current experiment and relaunching it to target that audience with targeted content offers based on the current article they are reading. This small nuance to the experiment is critical in knowing whether a targeted content offer based on the kind of content people consume helps increase donor conversion.

experiments.title: How removing an exit-intent pop-up on a donation page impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing the exit-intent pop-up impact conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/02/2018 - 01/26/2018
On Focus on the Family’s primary donation page, they use an exit-intent pop-up that appears on the page as you are about to leave. During their year-end giving campaign, we tested removing this feature. We found that by removing the exit-intent pop-up, we were able to increase donor conversion because the audience was highly motivated to give. Understanding these learnings, we also wanted to test this approach outside of year-end when motivations were more neutral. During the month of January, we tested removing the exit-intent pop-up from the primary donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings: After four weeks of running this experiment and analyzing the data, we found that having an exit-intent pop-up on the primary donation page doesn't significantly impact conversion - positively or negatively outside of high-urgency campaigns. The conversion rate throughout the month fluctuated week by week telling us that there is no real difference in conversion with an exit-intent pop-up.

experiments.title: How using identity-based messaging without a clear problem affected donor conversion on the main donation page

rows.name: Control - Improves Perception of the Problem

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that affirming the donor’s identity and shared values on the main donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion because people are more likely to take action when it reinforces their self-concept and aligns with their core beliefs.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/28/2025 - 08/05/2025
Focus on the Family’s main donation page is a critical point of conversion—but historically, it has resisted optimization, with most tests yielding no improvement. Our goal in this series of experiments is to uncover which messaging strategies can reliably lift donor response by better aligning the value proposition with what motivates giving.
In the previous test, we validated that problem-centric copy—focused on the family crisis and the donor’s role in addressing it—produced a meaningful increase in donor conversion, particularly in recurring gifts. Building on that insight, this second test explored a different psychological lever: identity alignment.
In this A/B test, the Control used the previous winning treatment—copy that framed the family crisis in vivid terms and emphasized the donor’s role in addressing urgent cultural and spiritual problems. The Treatment, by contrast, shifted to language that affirmed the donor’s identity, values, and motivations. It positioned the act of giving not just as a solution to a problem, but as a personal expression of belief and belonging—“You believe in biblical families. So do we.”
The A/B test ran on the primary donation page for users who clicked the main “Donate” button in site navigation. Traffic was evenly split. This is the second in a planned series of tests aimed at refining how Focus on the Family frames its impact—and understanding what kind of value proposition most effectively motivates giving.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test offered a valuable reminder of a core fundraising principle: donors are more likely to give when they clearly understand the problem and how their gift solves it.
The identity-based Treatment helped affirm the reader's shared beliefs and values, but it failed to clearly establish why support was urgently needed. It lacked the language about critical family struggles and the donor’s role in addressing them that made the Control language so compelling. As a result, recurring donor conversion dropped by 76%, with a 94.9% level of confidence. Other results were statistically flat, indicating no measurable lift in total or one-time giving.
This suggests that psychological distance may have widened in the Treatment. Without a concrete problem to solve, the act of giving may have felt more abstract or optional—especially for recurring support, which requires higher commitment. While identity alignment can enhance message resonance, it appears insufficient on its own to motivate action on a donation page without a compelling “why give now?”
Our key learning here then is that an effective appeal should try and incorporate both identity and clarity of the problem.
Next step:
We’ll test a hybrid value proposition that combines the emotional clarity of problem perception with the motivating power of identity alignment, to see if this blended approach can drive stronger overall conversion and deeper commitment.

experiments.title: How Adding More Value Prop to a Campaign Donation Page Affects Conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more value proposition to the donaton page for the See Life campaign will achieve a higher donor conversion because the value of what is on the page will increase.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/02/2024 - 05/15/2024
Focus on the Family runs a SeeLife campaign each year that receives a ton of traffic to its campaign donation page. The original campaign donation page was very short and did not have a ton of value proposition on it. We wondered if adding more value proposition, and length to the test could increase donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding more value proposition to the donation page for the See Life campaign did not result in a higher donor conversion. In fact, the treatment group actually saw a decrease in transactions for all traffic compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence is only 76.9%, which is below the standard threshold of 95%. This means that the results of this experiment cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to produce statistically significant results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test different variations of the donation page with varying levels of value proposition to see what resonates best with donors. It is also important to consider other factors that may influence donor conversion, such as the overall user experience, the donation process, and the clarity of messaging on the page. By conducting thorough and valid experiments, Focus on the Family can gather meaningful insights to optimize their donation pages and ultimately increase donor conversion in the future.

experiments.title: How making the donor the hero and using a empathetic, conversational tone on a content offer donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making the donor the hero and using a conversational, empathetic tone on a content offer donation page will increase donor conversion
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/24/2023 - 09/22/2023
Focus on the Family uses many free video series offers to acquire subscribers and donors. When reviewing their donation page template, we found that the copy was both long and impersonal. Its tone also talked a lot about the organization and less about the donor’s impact. It included phrases like, “we’ve done the math” that could potentially make visitors feel like Focus was the expert and their thoughts weren’t as valuable.
Users also mentioned that the template lacked a more authentic and conversational tone. We decided to redesign the donation page to be more impact-driven and conversational, hypothesizing that this would increase the perceived value of the offer while also making it easier to read.
We chose to run this on several offers. This experiment is for their Bringing Biblical Truth to a Broken Culture offer. You can review the results of a similar experiment on their Navigating Adolescence offer here.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for a month, we found no statistically significant impact on donor conversion from the treatment for this offer. We did however see an increase in donor conversion on the Navigating Adolescence offer we tested during the same time frame at a higher, though not quite valid level of confidence When looking at recurring donor conversion in the Broken Culture offer, we found a 34% increase but only at a 72% level of confidence so this wasn't valid. We will continue to test donation page layouts and language on a per-offer basis to see which designs will increase donor conversion.
Additional data can be found below:
One-Time Transactions: treatment led to a -1.6% decrease in donor conversion w/ a 11.9% LOCRecurring Transactions: treatment had a 37.9% increase in donor conversion w/ a 44.9% LOCVWO captured approximately $1,300 more revenue from the control than the treatment.

experiments.title: How concise copy and additional clarity impacted donor conversions for a content offer
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying the language, reordering information for a more logical flow, and emphasizing key points for visitors will achieve an increase in clarity and comprehension.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/05/2024 - 01/16/2024
In an effort to increase the donor conversion rate on IMB’s Prayer Calendar campaign, we decided to run a copy test to condense some of the copy. Our hypothesis was that the more concise the copy, the higher level of comprehension for the potential donor therefore, leading them to the donation form quicker.
We believe that simplifying the language, reordering information for a more logical flow, and emphasizing key points will lead to increased clarity and comprehension. As a result, we believe the treatment version will outperform the control version in terms of donor understanding and response, leading to a higher conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that simplifying the language, reordering information for a more logical flow, and emphasizing key points in the copy had a very slight lift in the number of transactions. While the treatment version of the campaign showed a 1.1% increase in transactions, the statistical level of confidence was only 3.1%, falling below the recommended threshold of 95% for validity.
Furthermore, we saw a a drop of 35.3% in revenue between the control and treatment at a 12.1% level of confidence. While this did not validate, it is important to consider in future decision making that IMB's donors give less when they are given less value proposition, and more concise copy may lead to a lower average gift.
In future experimentation, it is important to carry out the A/B test for a longer duration to gather a larger sample size and increase the statistical power. This will help establish a more reliable and accurate conclusion about the effectiveness of the treatment version. Additionally, it may be beneficial to further refine and optimize the treatment version based on the insights gained from this experiment. Overall, the learnings from this experiment should be used as a starting point for further iterations and testing to drive improved results.

experiments.title: How emphasizing the impact of a content offer on a landing page impacts downstream donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that emphasizing the impact the videos will have on subscribers will increase downstream donor conversion by increasing the perceived value of the videos.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/09/2023 - 06/30/2023
Focus on the Family uses dozens of different free video series offers to acquire new subscribers and donors. For most of these offers, their landing pages feature bullet points outlining the content of each episode in the series. In reviewing this standard design, we wanted to see if we could increase the appeal of the videos by using these bullet points to instead clarify what subscribers would be able to do as a result of these videos. This was inspired by Amy Harrison’s copywriting keynote at the 2022 NIO Summit.
We created a treatment version of their Cherish your Spouse video series landing page where the bullet points stated what subscribers would now be able to do, because of these short videos. We split traffic 50/50 and ran the experiment for 2 weeks. We believe that this new language would increase the perceived value of the videos, leading to more signups on the page and a higher downstream donor conversion on the subsequent donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After three weeks, we were unable to show any statistically significant impact, positive or negative, from the treatment. Revenue showed the biggest impact, but it too wasn't statistically valid. After testing impact bullet points on three offers, we had one with a clear increase in donor conversion, one with a clear decrease, and this experiment with no significant impact. This shows that the success of emphasizing the value/impact of an offer vs emphasizing the content of the offer depends on the impact that is emphasized. For this offer, emphasizing that the videos will enable you to "feel the closeness that comes from cherishing each other and experience greater marital intimacy" weren't compelling enough impacts to significantly impact conversion.

More testing is needed here offer by offer to identify the best value impacts to emphasize in the landing page copy.

experiments.title: How a value-driven headline on an acquisition offer landing page impacts email conversion, donor conversion, and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that incorporating reader-focused value proposition language for visitors to the Cherish video series landing page will achieve an increase in email conversions because the reader will have a better understanding of what the video series is and how it will help them in their own life. because the reader will have a better understanding of what the video series is and how it will help them in their own life.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/16/2023 - 06/10/2023
Focus on the Family is a Christian ministry that serves families worldwide. Every year, hundreds of thousands of people sign up for their free video series’ to learn more about strengthening marriages, families, and faith.
One of their most effective series is Cherish Your Spouse. In reviewing the landing page for this offer, we felt that the headline lacked a strong value for the visitor. We hypothesized that if we changed the headline to clarify the impact of the series on subscribers, it would increase the appeal of the offer and signups on the page, ultimately leading to more donations and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running this for three weeks, we found that there was no statistically significant impact on email signups from the new headline. Downstream donor conversion actually trended down for the treatment page but we were not able to validate that decrease either. Future tests will need to change more than the headline to have a higher likelihood of resulting in a statistically valid result. Our next test will be to change the body copy to emphasize what subscribers will experience as a result of watching these videos.

experiments.title: How an animation on the monthly giving option impacts conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an animated heart on the monthly donation tab for people who land on a video series acquisition donation page will achieve a higher number of monthly sustainers because the heart will draw the prospective donors attention to the monthly tab. because  the heart will draw the prospective donors attention to the monthly tab.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/24/2023 - 04/13/2023
Focus on the Family (FOTF) is a global ministry that receives tons of traffic through its acquisition video series. We wanted to see if we could increase the number of monthly sustainers that come from a video series by placing an animated heart on the monthly tab button on the donation form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding an animated heart on the monthly donation tab on a video series acquisition page resulted in a 7.2% increase in revenue for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 76.6% which means the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making.
Further testing will need to be done to see how we can increase the number of monthly sustainers from an acquisition offer.

experiments.title: How adding a high-urgency sticky bar to a recurring donor campaign page affects recurring donation rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a high-urgency sticky bar on the donation page of a recurring donor campaign will achieve higher recurring donation rates by defining a tangible goal and more clearly communicating the problem being solved.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/10/2023 - 03/31/2023
During a recurring donor campaign, we wanted to know how adding a high-urgency sticky bar to the donation page would affect recurring donation rates. The sticky bar combined high-urgency language with a dynamic countdown which automatically refreshed to display how many donors were still needed to reach the campaign goal. We set this experiment up as an A/B test with traffic split roughly 50/50 between the control and the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running this experiment for 21 days, we observed a 5.6% increase in recurring donations produced by the treatment but with a low statistical level of confidence (36.1%). Despite the modest lift in recurring donations, we also observed that the treatment produced a sizable 21% decrease in overall revenue (with a 75.5% level of confidence), indicating that by putting more emphasis on the number of donations, the treatment may have unexpectedly had a negative impact on the amount that donors were willing to give. It may therefore be worth testing this tactic against a monetary goal in the future.

experiments.title: How an exit-intent popup affects overall donation rates on a donation page during a recurring-giving campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that showing an exit-intent popup with a one-time ask for traffic attempting to exit a recurring-giving donation page will achieve higher overall donation rates by presenting a lower-commitment offer to leverage the reject and retreat principle of persuasion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/07/2023 - 03/17/2023
During a high-urgency campaign focused on acquiring new monthly donors, we wanted to know how an exit-intent popup would affect overall donation rates.
As the name applies, the exit-intent popup would populate only as a prospect attempted to leave the donation page without completing a donation. The specifics of the popup were designed to leverage the persuasion principle of reject and retreat. This principle is based on the idea of reciprocity and states that when someone is presented with a perceived large commitment and rejects the offer, they may be more likely to accept a subsequent offer if it has a perceived lower level of associated commitment—due to the fact that the prospect will be motivated to reciprocate the retreat by accepting the smaller offer.
In this case, the exit-intent popup would provide prospects who are attempting to leave the page without becoming a recurring donor the opportunity to still make an impact by instead giving a one-time gift of the same amount.
For this experiment, 50% of donation page traffic was shown the exit-intent popup while the other 50% did not see anything when they closed the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the treatment showing the exit-intent popup did produce a slight lift in overall donations, we are unable to draw any definitive conclusions about this experiment based on how the results were tracked.

Because the experiment session "fired" based on page load (rather than popup load) we are unable to determine whether it was the popup that persuaded more people to give or if these results occurred merely by chance. We were also only able to achieve a 39% level of confidence in these results. This does not mean that the tactic itself is invalid, just that in order to retest we will want to make sure the experiment "fires" only once someone see the popup and attempt to obtain a larger sample size to validate the results.

experiments.title: How a sticky bar with a “donors needed” countdown affects donation rates during a high-urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a sticky bar with a “donors still needed” countdown timer during a recurring donor high-urgency campaign will achieve donation rates because of increased urgency.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/02/2023 - 03/11/2023
In the context of a high-urgency campaign focused specifically on obtaining recurring monthly donors, we wanted to know how a sticky bar that dynamically populated a “donors still needed” countdown timer to increase urgency would affect donation rates.
The countdown was designed to automatically update donor counts (recurring donors only). We wanted to know how this would perform against the “wiki-banner” containing a simplified donation form that they have traditionally used.
We set this test up as a multi-variant experiment testing to versions of copy in the “countdown sticky”, with the control and both treatments all receiving roughly 1/3 of the traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for eight days, treatment A produced an 8.7% decrease in recurring donations (with a 30.7% level of confidence) and a 2.1% increase in overall donations (14.7% level of confidence); while treatment B produced a 7.2% increase in monthly donations (with a 24.6% level of confidence) and a .78% increase in overall donations (5.5% level of confidence). Because of these flat results and the unlikelihood of this experiment validating, we decided to end the experiment after just over a week.
We believe the low level of confidence was likely a result of running two treatments, while the flat results indicate that the wiki-banner's ability to produce a micro-commitment by having the prospective donor begin the process before landing on the donation page was motivating more or at least as many people to give.
However, we did make a significant observation while analyzing these results: for direct traffic—which accounted for the majority of traffic—treatment A did produce a 50.7% lift in overall donations (86.6% level of confidence) while treatment B produced a 26.4% lift in donations (58.6% level of confidence).
Because of this observation, we do believe that the urgency created by presenting a countdown of 'donors still needed' does play a role in inspiring people to give by at the very least prompting them to make a lesser commitment in the form of a one-time gift.
Therefore, we are planning to follow this experiment up with an A/B test that tests the current wiki-banner w/ donation form against a treatment with the higher-urgency copy and countdown functionality to see if combining the two will produce a significant lift.

experiments.title: How adding a privacy language to an acquisition offer donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a privacy statement underneath the donation button for visitors to the donation page will achieve a greater donor conversion rate because  the privacy statement adds perceived security to the form and reduces anxiety about how the donor’s information will be used.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/16/2023 - 02/24/2023
Focus on the Family (FOTF) is a global Christian ministry dedicated to helping families thrive. They run several different acquisition offers to attract donors on Facebook. We decided to run a series of tests to determine the impact of adding a statement promising to protect their privacy on the landing pages and donation pages.  This was the second test in the series where we examined the impact this disclaimer would have on their acquisition offer donation pages.
In our first test on the landing page, we saw that people who had the privacy and communication statement had a higher donor conversion rate than those who did not. We wanted to see if adding the same statement to the donation page would have the same effect. Could it raise donor conversion or even increase the average gift because of the added sense of security and transparency?
experiments.keyLearnings:
Adding the privacy statement under the donate button resulted in a significant increase in donor conversion and revenue.
Donor revenue increased by 63% with a 94% Level of Confidence. This could be due to adding the perceived sense of privacy, the transparency of what the donors are signing up for, or both.
The donor conversion rate increased as well by 37% with a 75% LOC, this experiment will need to be retested to see if the donor conversion rate would have been able to reach validity with a larger sample size.
The increase in donations observed during this experiment suggests that adding extra assurances and anticipating objections pertaining to how sensitive personal information is handled at the point of the transaction can have a positive effect on donation rates by reinforcing the security of the donation and conveying transparency in how the donor's information will be used.

experiments.title: How increasing perceived security at the point of transaction affects donation rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the perceived security at the point-of transaction on a main donation page (by explicitly stating how their payment details will be used) will increase donations by anticipating security concerns and increasing trust/transparency by reassuring potential donors that their sensitive information will be kept private.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/07/2023 - 03/03/2023
We know that online security is highly important to general web users, especially when it comes to entering sensitive data like payment information online. So we wanted to test how increasing perceived security at the point of transaction would impact donation rates on a main donation page for a family ministry organization.
In order to draw attention to the security statement below the donate button, we animated the lock icon to catch the eye of potential donors.
We also included a more detailed security statement to communicate that the donation is not only secure but that the donor’s sensitive payment information will also be kept private and will not be used for any other reason than to process the donation.
For this experiment, we split traffic evenly between the treatment explained above and the control which featured a static lock icon and a brief security statement, “Your information is secure.” for a duration of 21 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning gained from this experiment is that making security assurances more visible can significantly increase donations, validating our hypothesis that online security is highly important to web users, especially when it comes to entering their payment information.
This experiment shows that people need to feel secure when making donations and that focusing on reinforcing the security of their transaction will result in higher donation rates. We believe that the treatment anticipated and preemptively addressed a common objection among potential donors, thereby increasing trust and the desire to give to an organization that cares about donors by taking data privacy seriously.
After 21 days, this experiment saw a 26.3% increase in overall donations (99% level of confidence), with a 74.1% increase in revenue.
The treatment also produced a 37.4% increase in donations among desktop visitors (100% level of confidence)--who make up the majority of this organization's donor base.
Significantly, we also observed a 28.2% increase in donations among new visitors (96% level of confidence) and a 31% increase in donations among visitors referred from organic search (98% level of confidence), highlighting the importance of online security to the general population and among traffic that may be less familiar with the organization.
We are unable to draw a precise conclusion about whether the animated lock icon or the more detailed security statement played a more significant role in these results, however both variables apply to the stated strategy of "increasing perceived security" so we do not feel this invalidates the learning.
That being said, future experiments could focus on isolating these two variables and then continuing to refine the design of the donation page to make donors feel confident and secure when donating.

experiments.title: How communicating the amount of a donation goes to the organization’s mission impacts overall revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: How will including the percentage of a donor’s gift that goes directly towards the organization’s mission impact donor conversion and overall revenue?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/26/2021
Focus on the Family was sending an email appeal and recently adjusted the percentage of a donor’s gift amount that goes directly to ministry versus overhead cost for their organization. Historically, they had never communicated that to their donors. With the new higher percentage of the donor’s gift going directly to their organization, they wanted to test adding this message to the body copy of the donation page to see if they might be able to increase donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: While there was no valid lift in donor conversion, we did see a directional increase in overall revenue of 33%! By communicating to people that we are being good stewards of their money and that their gift now goes even further towards ministry impact, donors were even more generous and gave larger gifts than when we didn't communicate that to them. This tells us that this principle is important to donors, but that it doesn't impact the number of donors who convert, but instead it does impact the generosity of their gift.

experiments.title: How showing an abandoned donation pop-up to users based on entry point affects revenue – homepage version
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that visitors entering the site on the main page will be prompted to complete their donation when presented a pop-up reminder and we will generate more revenue than not showing a pop-up.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/11/2022
Focus on the Family wanted to see if showing a pop-up with an opportunity to complete a donation (from a previously clicked on amount), would change based on the entry point of a visitor. The thought was that user intentions might be different for visitors coming directly to the home page vs visitors entering from other pages.
Prior to this experiment, we first tested if showing a pop-up to visitors (regardless of entry point) would increase overall completed transactions. We built this initial test with a single pop up that was shown to all visitors that visited a donation page, clicked on an amount to give, but abandoned the process after that. The pop-up would show to that visitor as they navigated around on the FOTF site.
For this test, we split the test into two separate tests. One test focussed on visitors to all non-homepages. The pop up was shown only to folks that came through anywhere but the main page, hit the donate button, clicked an amount…then abandoned the process.
This particular test focused on visitors to the main home page. The pop up behaved exactly the same, except that it only showed to people that visited through the homepage page first. The idea was that people coming through main page would be more familiar and thus more likely to complete a transaction, but they may need more reminders if they happened to go through the donation pathway…and possibly the abandoned donation pop up would serve as that gentle nudge and cause a completed transaction, and thus more revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By showing a pop-up reminder to complete the transaction, our theory was that those that came to the main page to make a donation, but abandoned for some reason, would potentially be swayed by this gentle nudge. Just like the test on the non-homepage version, When we pulled transaction data, we found quite a few gifts that skewed the results of both the control and the treatment. We decided to remove any donation of $2k or more from both the treatment and the control in order to remove any outliers that might be skewing the results. When we did this, we found the data to be inconclusive with an 11% decrease in the treatment, but with only a 53% Level of Confidence. There is no real positive or negative effect on the results. It is also important to note that experiments for FOTF that run outside of high-urgency time frames behave much differently than during campaign periods.

The key takeaway is that when running transaction experiments that involve revenue, we cannot take the results on face value. We must dig into the numbers deeper, look for any anomalies that can skew results, and then evaluate for lift. In this case, while we did not see a real difference either way (after removing outlier data), we also did not see a potentially harmful outcome. While we cannot recommend that this current variation become the new control, we can recommend to keep testing different variations of the same experiment. Next up is a reduced ask (60% of the originally clicked donation amount), but only on the non-homepage version.

experiments.title: How showing an abandoned donation pop-up to users based on entry point affects revenue – non-homepage version
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that visitors entering the site outside of the main page will be prompted to complete their donation when presented a pop-up reminder and we will generate more revenue than not showing a pop-up.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/03/2022
Focus on the Family wanted to see if showing a pop-up with an opportunity to complete a donation (from a previously clicked on amount), would change based on the entry point of a visitor. The thought was that user intentions might be different for visitors coming directly to the home page vs visitors entering from other pages.
Prior to this experiment, we first tested if showing a pop-up to visitors (regardless of entry point) would increase overall completed transactions. We built this initial test with a single pop up that was shown to all visitors that visited a donation page, clicked on an amount to give, but abandoned the process after that. The pop-up would show to that visitor as they navigated around on the FOTF site.
For this test, we split the test into two separate tests. One test focussed on visitors to the main page. The pop up was shown only to folks that came through the main page, hit the donate button, clicked an amount…then abandoned the process. This particular test focused on visitors to all other pages. The pop up behaved exactly the same, except that it only showed to people that visited through non-homepage pages first. The idea was that people coming through main page would be more familiar and thus more likey to complete a transaction, where as visitors that came to article pages or via a SEO search, may need more reminders if they happened to go through the donation pathway…and possibly the abandoned donation pop up would serve as that gentle nudge and cause a completed transaction, and thus more revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test provided some interesting early data. Our hypothesis was that motivations for people visiting the site outside of the main page would be lower to donate than those from main site visits. By showing a pop-up reminder to complete the transaction, our theory was that those that had less motivation would potentially be swayed by this gentle nudge. After about 10 days of running the experiment, the test actually validated (with a greater than 95% LoC) based on revenue. The test results also showed that average gift for the treatment was substantially higher (over $25 greater for the treatment). While these initial results were very encouraging, whenever we run a test on revenue lift, we always need to look for outlier donations that could potentially skew the data results. When we pulled transaction data, we found quite a few gifts that skewed the results of both the control and the treatment. There was a $20k gift on the control, and several $5k plus gifts on the treatment. We decided to remove any donation of $1k or more from both the treatment and the control in order to remove any outliers that might be skewing the results. When we did this, we found the data to be inconclusive. There is no real positive or negative effect on the results. It is also important to note that experiments for FOTF that run outside of high-urgency time frames behave much differently than during campaign periods.
The key takeaway is that when running transaction experiments that involve revenue, we cannot take the results on face value. We must dig into the numbers deeper, look for any anomalies that can skew results, and then evaluate for lift. In this case, while we did not see a real difference either way (after removing outlier data), we also did not see a potentially harmful outcome. While we cannot recommend that this current variation become the new control, we can recommend to keep testing different variations of the same experiment. Next up is a reduced ask (60% of the originally clicked donation amount).

experiments.title: How redirecting mobile traffic to a specific section of a donation form affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing friction in the donation pathway for highly motivated users during a high urgency campaign will result in greater donor conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/13/2022 - 06/10/2022
Focus on the Family runs an annual high-urgency campaign for their pro-life movement called SeeLife. During this campaign, they run a sticky banner atop all of their pages that contains a specific call to action with a donation amount on the banner. There is little to no value proposition on this banner (especially on a mobile device), just a very simple donation ask, accompanied by specific donation amounts. When clicking on this banner, the user is normally redirected to the top of a campaign donation page where they can watch a video about the campaign, read more about it, then scroll down to make a donation.
For the purposes of this experiment, Focus on the Family wanted to test if redirecting the user to an anchored location on the donation form embedded on the campaign donation page would result in a higher donor conversion. This test would bypass the content at the top of the page, and give the donor the option to make their donation quickly…especially on a mobile device where screen real estate is limited. In addition, the team wanted to test if an anchored re-direct vs. a locked redirect (where the user had to initiate a force click out of the locked screen) would result in a higher conversion.
The working theory was that a user was already highly motivated to give due to the nature of the high urgency campaign, so removing as much friction as possible could lead to higher donor conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
As expected, the treatments for this user group experienced a lift. While the experiment did not validate due to a small sample size and a low level of confidence, the experiment did provide some good learnings. There was an 8.1% lift in conversion rate for the locked version with a 57% level of confidence, and a 3.2% lift with 25% level of confidence for the anchored version. Both treatments saw a positive lift over the control. However, we would like to try a similar experiment during a time when the user was not as motivated by a high urgency campaign to see if this would have a similar result. We would recommend rolling this out again for other high-urgency campaigns that have similar circumstances.

Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 37.2% increase with a 87% level of confidence.
Paid Search visitors had a 136.3% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How adding social proof and more clarity to a landing page affects conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more clarity to the headline and adding social proof in the form of a testimonial would result in a higher number of email conversions for people that land on this campaign landing page
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/14/2022 - 06/28/2022
Focus on the Family has a number of free video series on topics around Faith, Marriage, and Parenting. The goal of putting out these free video series is to share free content around these topics and to collect names and email addresses of interested subscribers and then eventually nurture them towards a donation to the mission. The process is to promote the free video series on social media campaign, drive traffic to a landing page where they collect info like email address and name to send a link to access the free series, then upon a successful info collection, immediately send the subscriber to an instant donation page where they have the opportunity to provide a gift.
As we evaluate opportunities for optimization all along the marketing funnel, we look for metrics that show areas where we can make improvements in traffic conversion (CPMs, CTRs, CPCs), email conversions (Signup conversion rates), and instant donation conversions (ICRs). For this particular experiment, this email collection landing page is part of the overall campaign strategy and what we would refer to as the “middle of the marketing funnel”. The signup conversion rates on this campaign were lower than average, so we felt there was an opportunity to make improvements.
We decided to test if adding social proof in the form of a testimonial, and more clarity in the headline would result in a higher number of email signup conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for a short duration being promoted through a social media campaign to get traffic. This campaign (like all Focus on the Family campaigns) is evaluated on a weekly basis for performance against a lofty ROI equation that requires a 1.1 ROI in order to continue getting paid media allocation and thus traffic to potentially validate. While there was not enough sample size to validate the experiment, we did see a small lift of around a 27% for the treatment.

Due to the condensed timeframe for these experiments from media allocation, we experimented with two variants (headline clarity and social proof). While we typically like to limit experiments to 1 variable to isolate learnings, we opted for two variants in the same experiment to give a better opportunity for lift. With a low Level of Confidence (LoC - around 28%) and a small sample size, we cannot say definitively if the increased headline clarity or the social proof in the form of a testimonial were the cause of the lift. We can say that we did see an increase in conversions, and gained learnings that we can continue to apply to future landing pages.

For future experiments with longer run times, we will try to isolate variants into single treatments for better learning.

experiments.title: How aligning the messaging of a landing page to recent news events affects the conversion rate of page that has historically performed well
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding recency copy to the headline and body copy for an email sign up page will achieve a higher number of conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/14/2022 - 08/14/2022
Focus on the Family has a number of free video series on topics around Faith, Marriage, and Parenting. The goal of putting out these free video series is to share free content around these topics and to collect names and email addresses of interested subscribers and then eventually nurture them towards a donation to the mission. The process is to promote the free video series on social media campaign, drive traffic to a landing page where they collect info like email address and name to send a link to access the free series, then upon a successful info collection, immediately send the subscriber to an instant donation page where they have the opportunity to provide a gift.
As we evaluate opportunities for optimization all along the marketing funnel, we look for metrics that show areas where we can make improvements in traffic conversion (CPMs, CTRs, CPCs), email conversions (Signup conversion rates), and instant donation conversions (ICRs). For this particular experiment, this email collection landing page is part of the overall campaign strategy and what we would refer to as the “middle of the marketing funnel”. The signup conversion rates on this campaign had been steady for a long time, as this offer has been part of the Focus on the Family ecosystem for quite some time, but there is always room for improvement.
Due to recent tragic events that transpired at the time of this experiment, we felt we could align the messaging of this offer to the headlines of the day…without specifically calling out the tragedy itself. We wanted the messaging to remain evergreen, even if the treatment won out. We decided to test if adding recency copy to the headline and body copy would result in a higher number of email signup conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for a short duration being promoted through a social media campaign to get traffic. This campaign (like all Focus on the Family campaigns) is evaluated on a weekly basis for performance against a lofty ROI equation that requires a 1.1 ROI in order to continue getting paid media allocation and thus traffic to potentially validate.  While there was not enough sample size to validate the experiment, we did see a 38% lift in email conversions for the treatment, with a 52% level of confidence.
With a low Level of Confidence (LoC - around 52%) and a small sample size, we cannot say definitively if the recency copy that aligned with the news headlines of the time would have continued to outperform the control. We can say that we did see an increase in conversions during the short timeframe, and gained learnings that we can continue to apply to future landing pages.
The copy points, while aligned with the recent headlines, still remain evergreen enough to keep live, and could continue to see gains in conversions if we make this treatment the new control.

experiments.title: How aligning the graphic elements from an ad with the graphic elements on a landing page affect conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that matching the creative assets from the ad running on social media to the graphics on the landing page will result in higher conversions for visitors to the email landing page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/14/2022 - 08/14/2022
Focus on the Family has a number of free video series on topics around Faith, Marriage, and Parenting. The goal of putting out these free video series is to share free content around these topics and to collect names and email addresses of interested subscribers and then eventually nurture them towards a donation to the mission. The process is to promote the free video series on social media campaign, drive traffic to a landing page where they collect info like email address and name to send a link to access the free series, then upon a successful info collection, immediately send the subscriber to an instant donation page where they have the opportunity to provide a gift.
As we evaluate opportunities for optimization all along the marketing funnel, we look for metrics that show areas where we can make improvements in traffic conversion (CPMs, CTRs, CPCs), email conversions (Signup conversion rates), and instant donation conversions (ICRs).
It is common for this team to update the ad creative in Facebook if we see that the ad conversion rates could use improvement. It is not as common to also have the graphics from the campaign match exactly the images on the landing page. While the change in creative helped the ad conversion rates at the “top of the funnel”, we noticed the signup conversion rates on this campaign (in the “middle of the funnel”) began to decline after making the creative switch. We felt this was an opportunity to test creative congruence between the new ad creative and the existing landing page.
For this particular experiment, in the treatment, we changed out creative assets on the email sign up page to match that of the new ad creative running on Facebook, and we made a slight tweak to the headline copy to add more clarity. The control was left as is.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for a short duration being promoted through a social media campaign to get traffic. This campaign (like all Focus on the Family campaigns) is evaluated on a weekly basis for performance against a lofty ROI equation that requires a 1.1 ROI in order to continue getting paid media allocation and thus traffic to potentially validate. While there was not enough sample size to validate the experiment, we saw very little difference between the control and the treatment. In fact, the control (with a different graphic treatment between the ad and the landing page) was slightly ahead on email conversions (by just 3).

Due to the condensed timeframe for these experiments from media allocation, we experimented with two variants (headline clarity and graphic treatment). While we typically like to limit experiments to 1 variable to isolate learnings, we opted for two variants in the same experiment to give a better opportunity for lift. With a low Level of Confidence (LoC - around 10%) and a small sample size, we cannot say definitively if the increased headline clarity or the graphic treatment caused the small decrease in conversions. We can say that we gained learnings that we can continue to apply to future landing pages. In the end, there were really no positive or adverse effects to making the creative match.

For future experiments with longer run times, we will try to isolate variants into single treatments for better learning.

experiments.title: How adding more clarity to a landing page headline affects sign up conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more clarity into the headline for readers that land on this page will achieve a higher number of email conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/14/2022 - 08/14/2022
Focus on the Family has a number of free video series on topics around Faith, Marriage, and Parenting. The goal of putting out these free video series is to share free content around these topics and to collect names and email addresses of interested subscribers and then eventually nurture them towards a donation to the mission. The process is to promote the free video series on social media campaign, drive traffic to a landing page where they collect info like email address and name to send a link to access the free series, then upon a successful info collection, immediately send the subscriber to an instant donation page where they have the opportunity to provide a gift.
As we evaluate opportunities for optimization all along the marketing funnel, we look for metrics that show areas where we can make improvements in traffic conversion (CPMs, CTRs, CPCs), email conversions (Signup conversion rates), and instant donation conversions (ICRs). For this particular experiment, this email collection landing page is part of the overall campaign strategy and what we would refer to as the “middle of the marketing funnel”. The signup conversion rates on this campaign were lower than average, so we felt there was an opportunity to make improvements.
We decided to test if adding more clarity in the headline would result in a higher number of email signup conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for a short duration being promoted through a social media campaign to get traffic. This campaign (like all Focus on the Family campaigns) is evaluated on a weekly basis for performance against a lofty ROI equation that requires a 1.1 ROI in order to continue getting paid media allocation and thus traffic to potentially validate.  While there was not enough sample size to validate the experiment, we saw a decrease of around a 27% for the treatment, with a 27% level of confidence.
Due to the condensed timeframe for these experiments from media allocation, we experimented with two variants (headline clarity and increased emphasis on the call to action text - not shown in the screenshot).   While we typically like to limit experiments to 1 variable to isolate learnings, we opted for two variants in the same experiment to give a better opportunity for lift. With a low Level of Confidence (LoC - around 27%) and a small sample size, we cannot say definitively if the increased headline clarity or the increased emphasis on the CTA were the cause of the drop in conversions. We can say that we saw a decrease in conversions and gained learnings that we can continue to apply to future landing pages.
For future experiments with longer run times, we will try to isolate variants into single treatments for better learning.

experiments.title: How adding social proof and headline clarity affect email conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding social proof and headline clarity  for readers of a landing page will achieve a higher number of conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/07/2022 - 04/10/2022
Focus on the Family has a number of free video series on topics around Faith, Marriage, and Parenting. The goal of putting out these free video series is to share free content around these topics and to collect names and email addresses of interested subscribers and then eventually nurture them towards a donation to the mission. The process is to promote the free video series on social media campaign, drive traffic to a landing page where they collect info like email address and name to send a link to access the free series, then upon a successful info collection, immediately send the subscriber to an instant donation page where they have the opportunity to provide a gift.
As we evaluate opportunities for optimization all along the marketing funnel, we look for metrics that show areas where we can make improvements in traffic conversion (CPMs, CTRs, CPCs), email conversions (Signup conversion rates), and instant donation conversions (ICRs). For this particular experiment, this email collection landing page is part of the overall campaign strategy and what we would refer to as the “middle of the marketing funnel”. The signup conversion rates on this campaign were lower than average, so we felt there was an opportunity to make improvements.
We decided to test if adding social proof under a landing page image and more clarity in the headline would result in a higher number of email signup conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for a short duration being promoted through a social media campaign to get traffic. This campaign (like all Focus on the Family campaigns) is evaluated on a weekly basis for performance against a lofty ROI equation that requires a 1.1 ROI in order to continue getting paid media allocation and thus traffic to potentially validate.  While there was not enough sample size to validate the experiment, we did see a small lift of around 2% for the treatment with a 20% level of confidence.
Due to the condensed timeframe for these experiments from media allocation, we experimented with two variants (headline clarity and social proof).   While we typically like to limit experiments to 1 variable to isolate learnings, we opted for two variants in the same experiment to give a better opportunity for lift. With a low Level of Confidence (LoC - around 20%) and a small sample size, we cannot say definitively if the increased headline clarity or the social proof were the cause of the lift. We can say that we did see an increase in conversions, and gained learnings that we can continue to apply to future landing pages.
For future experiments with longer run times, we will try to isolate variants into single treatments for better learning.

experiments.title: How removing a video on a donation page affects donor conversion during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a campaign donation page that does NOT feature a promotional video will convert more potential donors than a page that DOES feature a video.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/12/2022 - 05/16/2022
Focus on the Family has an annual high urgency campaign called See Life to help fund their Option Ultrasound program. This annual campaign has a $4 million dollar donor match fund and attracts a highly motivated potential donor base. With this campaign, Focus on the Family wanted to test whether or not having a video on the donation page caused any additional friction for getting visitors to make a donation. Their control featured a 3 minute promotional video for the Option Ultrasound program just above the donation form. The treatment removed the video from the donation page all together.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for just over a month, and saw both an increase for the treatment (no video) and a decrease for the treatment over the course of 4 weeks, before finally settling on a 1.1% increase in conversion rate for the treatment by the time the experiment ended. Due to a lack of sample size (19k samples vs the required 491k samples) and a low level of confidence (21.4%), this experiment did not validate.
