This shows that having a video on this particular campaign donation page had no relative difference in the donation intentions for the majority of visitors.
Of particular note is that running this experiment for a campaign that has a very high donation intention could potentially skew the results. We suggest running this experiment again for a campaign that does not have a donor base that is as highly motivated as this one. This might provide better learning as to whether or not showing a video on a donation page either helps or hinders the number of donors that actually make a gift.
Also of note, while revenue experienced a 55.6% increase with a 99% level of confidence, this number was skewed by a couple of large individual donations.

experiments.title: How adding an impact statement under the donate button affects conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an impact statement under the donate button for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/18/2021 - 02/11/2022
Previous experiments have shown that showing donors the direct impact of their donations may inspire them to give, decreasing abandonment and improving conversions rates.
We wanted to leverage this learning in an effort to improve flat conversion rates on a donation page where visitors land after signing up for one of Focus on the Family’s video series. For this experiment, we developed a treatment that included an impact statement directly below the donate button to bring more attention to the value of their donation. We hypothesized that the inclusion of a short statement telling donors what their donations have achieved would motivate them to give, reducing abandonment and improving conversion rates by putting the donor center-stage. For this experiment, we ran an A/B test with traffic split 50/50.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 82 days, we observed a 11.9% lift in donor conversion rate with 69% level of confidence so the results are not valid. However, when we looked at how individual segments responses, we found that returning visitors had a 207% increase in conversions with 100% level of confidence, hinting that including an impact statement with donor-centric language near the donate button can provide donors with the extra inspiration they need to give by illustrating the importance and effectiveness of their donation.
It was also interesting to discover that while desktop visitors had a 181.4% increase in donations with 99% level of confidence, mobile visitors had a 14.9% decrease in donations with 79.0% level of confidence. This is an important observation and could indicate a shallower scroll-depth for mobile users. This may warrant a future test that moves the value proposition above the donation button on mobile devices.
The promising results of this experiment further reinforce that adding value proposition at the point of final decision making - when the donor clicks the make my gift button - can help highlight the impact of their donation and inspire them to complete the giving process.

experiments.title: How writing a more detailed headline affected email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that detailing the offer format in the headline for landing page visitors will achieve greater email acquisition.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/10/2022 - 03/30/2022
In an effort to improve email acquisition on the sign-up page for one of Focus on the Family’s video series, we decided to create a treatment with a more detailed headline that explained the format of the content the visitor would receive when they signed up.
The headline used in the control did an adequate job of explaining the benefit gained by signing up, but it did not specify that the visitor was signing up for a video series. Because previous experiments have shown that specificity can have a positive impact on conversions, and knowing that in 2020 in the US reached 244.4 million people watched digital videos online, we decided to create a treatment headline that included how the benefit would be gained—in the form of a free video series. For this experiment, we ran an A/B test with traffic split 50/50.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 48 days, we observed a .5% lift in sign-ups. The low sample size of this experiment prevented us from validating our hypothesis that a more detailed headline would increase the number of visitors who signed up for the video series.
Nevertheless, the low lift in sign-ups does indicate that it was the benefit gained from the video series that appealed most to visitors. The details of the offer (free video series) are also mentioned early in the body copy and so this inclusion in the headline may have been redundant.
For now we can conclude that focusing on the problem the visitor wants to solve in the headline is an effective way to add value to an offer.
Next steps may be to further expand on this benefit, hitting on an emotional pain point by including what this benefit (winning inner battle) means for the visitor, ie. Learn how to win the battle with yourself, so that {how winning the inner battle improves your life}.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign of a landing page with a seasonal offer impacted email conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a radical redesign for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in email conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/12/2021 - 04/08/2021
In the weeks leading up to Easter, a sticky bar on the homepage was pointing visitors to a landing page where they could sign up for a free resource offer with Easter-focused family activities. We wanted to test a radical redesign of the landing page to see if we could increase overall email acquisition on this offer. We developed a treatment that removed the traditional footer with links to alternate pages on the site to avoid distraction from the intended call to action, which was to submit the email acquisition form and sign up for the free PDF.
We also revised the header on the page from “Adventures in Odyssey: Easter Faith Building Activities!” to “Get access to your free copy now and help your family celebrate Easter together with this free PDF of fun ideas for activities and devotionals to prompt meaningful conversations!” in an effort to make the offer less organization-focused and more focused on the page visitor.
The third element we changed for the treatment version of the page was enhancing the overall value proposition and added in language that helped address the why behind someone might benefit from the contents of the free guide rather than only sharing what was inside. We spoke directly to the felt needs of the audience and the emotions they might be experiencing as a parent and how this resource could alleviate some of the stress that often accompanies the anticipation of the Easter holiday.
Finally, we changed the CTA on the form from “To get your FREE download of Easter Faith Building Activities, fill out the form below” to “Tell us where to send your FREE copy of Adventure in Odyssey’s Easter Faith Building Activities:” and switched the button copy from “Download Here” to “Send my free copy »” to make this portion feel like less work for the user. We set up the control and the treatment to run as an A/B test and split traffic to each version.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Since this was a limited time offer, we didn't have enough run-time to acquire the number of visitors to the page needed to validate results and we only reached a 35% level of confidence on the experiment. However, what we saw was a few observations on specific visitor segments that are outlined below. Even though we did not reach statistical significance overall, we can use these learnings to guide the development of language for future tests that target display/tablet visitors and speak more relevantly to females and those who are 65+. We could also use this knowledge to our advantage by keeping this audience in mind for introducing social proof on a landing page by adding a testimonial quote from a 65+ female who found the resource valuable. We can also test each of these items from the radical redesign individually on other free offers in the future to have more insight into which changes led to these increases and decreases per segment.
Desktop visitors had a 31.7% decrease in outbound link click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Display visitors had a 42.1% increase in outbound link click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 34.9% decrease in outbound link click with a 94% level of confidence.
 	Tablet visitors had a 33.1% increase in outbound link click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Organic Search visitors had a 28.5% decrease in outbound link click with a 95% level of confidence.
 	Referral visitors had a 32.0% decrease in outbound link click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Female visitors had a 39.5% increase in outbound link click with a 98% level of confidence.
 	Direct visitors had a 100.0% decrease in outbound link click with a 99% level of confidence.
 	Social visitors had a 100.0% decrease in outbound link click with a 97% level of confidence.
 	25-34 visitors had a 72.0% decrease in outbound link click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	35-44 visitors had an 86.2% decrease in outbound link click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	65+ visitors had a 1,211.5% increase in outbound link click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	18-24 visitors had a 34.0% increase in outbound link click with a 100% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How strengthening the value proposition of the wiki banner impacts donor conversion during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: Can increasing the clarity of the value proposition on the wiki banner increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/13/2021 - 07/15/2021
Focus on the Family uses a banner at the top of their website during high urgency campaigns to capture site traffic and convert them into donors. The messaging for this banner is always in alignment with the campaign messaging. It usually includes a headline and a direct call to action. We hypothesized that we could increase donor conversion by increasing the clarity of the value proposition in the banner. To do this, we developed a treatment that communicated the problem and presented a call-to-action as the solution.
experiments.keyLearnings:
For all traffic, there was no positive or negative impact on donor conversion. That said, this neutral result is due to the significant differences in impact with opposing segments. Specifically, when looking at new visitors to the site, there was a 4% decrease in conversion; while with returning visitors, there was a 5% increase in donor conversion. What we can learn from this experiment is that the motivation of users on the site are wildly different and the additional value proposition is needed for some site visitors, but not all. This can be dependent upon other messaging they have seen in other channels, and even people's familiarity with the organization. One specific trend we saw in the data is that when people were coming from an outside channel where they may have read or seen messaging for the campaign, the increased value proposition helped to increase donor conversion when people got to the page because the message they had previously read was reinforced through the wiki banner on the top of the website.
Other observations of the impact on visitor segments in the experiment:
Direct visitors had a 14.7% decrease in donations with a 100% level of confidence.Email visitors had a 16.3% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.Paid Search visitors had a 47.3% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.Social visitors had a 74.8% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.Female visitors had a 35.7% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.Male visitors had a 36.0% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.New  visitors had a 4.1% decrease in donations with a 93% level of confidence.Returning  visitors had a 4.7% increase in donations with a 97% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How presenting the gift frequency as a tabbed form on acquisition pages impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a tabbed form have a negative impact on donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/09/2021 - 07/11/2021
Focus on the Family has continued to test into the best way to present the gift frequency/gift type section on their donation pages. After learning that presenting it as a tabbed form increased donor conversion by 16% on the primary donation page, they wanted to test this method on other donation pages to ensure that it didn’t have a negative impact on other donation pages so that they could potentially roll this approach out across all donation pages. To test into this, they ran the tabbed form across some of their acquisition donation pages.
experiments.keyLearnings: The tabbed form had neither a positive or negative impact on donor conversion. This is a great learning because it confirms that the tabbed approach can be rolled out across all donation pages as the approach to use for presenting the gift frequency/gift kind section.

experiments.title: How increasing clarity that a content offer is free affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making it clear a content offer is free, while tying that to value proposition, will not decrease giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/14/2021 - 07/29/2021
Focus on the Family had been receiving complaints that people didn’t know the video series was free and they believed they needed to make a donation to get the series. We tested one iteration of a donation page that increased the use of the word free in conjunction with the video series. That test decreased donations by 20%. We then tested another iteration of a donation page that focused on clarity and addressed the reader specifically, rather than increasing the reference to the series being free. This did not have a negative impact on donor conversion and only brought more clarity to the page – which was what we had hoped. To make sure that test wasn’t an isolated experience, we tested that exact same approach on one of the most popular video series to see the impact.
This was a 50/50 A/B test that ran for four weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings: This iteration of the test didn't decrease donations while it increased clarity that the reader did not have to donate to receive the video series. These results are consistent with the same test we ran on another offer. We will roll this approach out to all acquisition pages.

experiments.title: How a redesign of a newsletter offer impacted clicks from the homepage
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value proposition for homepage visitors will achieve more clicks to sign up for a newsletter.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/07/2021 - 08/02/2021
As we evaluated the Adventures in Odyssey homepage, we wanted to find a way to increase the number of people who were clicking on the link to sign up for the newsletter. We wanted to develop a treatment that increased clarity about what the visitor would receive through the newsletter, emphasized the appeal by highlighting the benefit and value of getting access to the content rather than not signing up at all, and indicated the offer was unique, exclusive, and why it is distinct from signing up for any other newsletter. We took the control headline “Sign up for our Newsletter” and replaced it with “Get access to the latest resources for your family”. We also revised the supporting body copy from “Sign up to receive the monthly Adventures in Odyssey newsletter directly to your inbox” to “We’d love to share our most recent and helpful resources with you and your family! Be inspired to grow in your faith through these exclusive updates you won’t find anywhere else!”. And the final element we changed was the button text which read “Subscribe Now” while the treatment version said “Get Access Now”. We split traffic to each version 50/50 and let the experiment run.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We were not able to reach statistical significance on the experiment, but the control saw a 20.8% increase with a 72.5% level of confidence when compared to the results of the treatment. What's particularly interesting is when reviewing the segments, we had a significant portion of samples that were visiting from mobile and who were labeled as "new visitors" but both of these segments show lower conversion rates, so future tests for this page could be more strategically designed to address these types of visitors since they make up the majority of the traffic volume. It also appears that returning visitors are more likely to click to sign up for the newsletter so we could potentially develop additional prompts such as a pop-up on someone's second or third visit that highlights the offer more prominently once we know they have shown a continued interest in the content. Phase two of this experiment involves testing the landing page copy where people end up once someone has clicked the button to subscribe so we can find out if people are actually converting and entering their email address to get the newsletter on the second step of the process after they have clicked through from the homepage.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Tablet visitors had a 100.0% decrease in newsletter clicks with a 95% level of confidence.New visitors had a 100.0% decrease in newsletter clicks with a 100% level of confidence.Returning visitors had a 131.9% increase in newsletter clicks with a 100% level of confidence.(Other) visitors had a 100.0% decrease in newsletter clicks with a 97% level of confidence.Direct visitors had an 83.4% decrease in newsletter clicks with a 100% level of confidence.Organic Search visitors had a 100.0% decrease in newsletter clicks with a 100% level of confidence.Referral visitors had a 100.0% decrease in newsletter clicks with a 100% level of confidence.Social visitors had a 100.0% decrease in newsletter clicks with a 92% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How an alternate form of social proof impacted email acquisition for free trial sign-ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that incorporating additional social proof for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in email acquisition for an offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/02/2021 - 03/15/2021
The long-standing “winner” (in terms of highest conversion rates) sticky bar on the OA Club homepage included a testimonial quote from one user, so we knew that social proof works well for this audience, however we wanted to test if using a different form of the concept of social proof would hold this pattern. We thought that if we could broaden the social proof experiment to apply to more visitors and demonstrate stronger credibility through a third-party then we would see an increase in free trial signups. So we launched a treatment to the sticky bar that said “Join over 140,000 families who have helped their children grow in their faith and learn biblical truths over the past year with a free 14-day trial of Adventures in Odyssey!”
experiments.keyLearnings:
Unfortunately, we had to end the experiment early to launch another timebound offer on this same page so we did not have enough time to generate the volume of traffic needed to reach validity and saw a 2.8% decrease in conversion rate with just a 21.5% level of confidence. We will want to relaunch the experiment when we have the opportunity to leave it running long enough to get an appropriate amount of samples to analyze. However, over the 10 days the experiment ran, we did observe the following impacts on other visitor segments in the experiment that could help with more specific targeting on future treatments that are developed:
Female visitors had a 113.8% increase in begin free trial signup with a 99% level of confidence.18-24 visitors had a 98.4% increase in begin free trial signup with an 88% level of confidence.Tablet visitors had a 79.1% increase in begin free trial signup with a 94% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How including author information affects video series sign up rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Including information on the author/speaker will increase signups and email acquisition.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/26/2021 - 05/03/2021
Focus on the Family was releasing a new video series and they wanted to see what the impact of including information on the speaker would be on email conversion. The thought was that the broad audience that lands on the sign up page may not recognize the name of the speaker and so knowing more about them would convince the readers to sign up. This was a 50/50 split test and the primary traffic was from social media.
(N.B. The client set up the variable page and because of the nature of the test set up, the variable shows as the control.)
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment did not affect conversion rate with any valid level of confidence either way. However, we do not think that means including information for the speaker is not helpful to convincing the reader to sign up. We do think that the placing the information below the decision point of the submission button made the information a second thought and so did not have an impact.

experiments.title: How testimonials from previous series viewers affect email conversion rate on an acquisition page
experiments.researchQuestion: Showing the value of the offer through the words of users will lend credibility to the series, increase its perceived value, and so increase the email conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/10/2021 - 03/14/2021
Focus’s video series Raising Your Kids to Defend Their Faith had a low conversion rate. So we tested placing testimonials from parents who used the series on the page. This ran for over a month. Traffic was primarily from acquisition ads on social media.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment did not affect conversion rate with any valid level of confidence. However, we do not think that means testimonials are not helpful to convincing the reader to sign up. We do think that this shows that the placement and these testimonials on this offer did not move the needle for readers. Further testing of placement and copy is needed.

experiments.title: How personalizing the donation page with the reader’s first name impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that speaking to someone using their first name for visitors to the donation page will achieve higher donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/27/2021 - 04/09/2021
Focus had a new offer running around Biblical truth in a broken world. It had a strong conversion rate and a lot of traffic. So we tested a personalization by passing the person’s first name from the landing page to the donation page and adding it to the header. Instead of just saying “Congratulations!” it said “Congratulations, John!” This has worked well in the past for other organizations and we wanted to test it for the Focus audience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
What we found was the opposite of expected. Using the reader's first name actually decreased donations by 36% and revenue by 37% with a directional decrease. This level of decrease held true across almost all devices and demographics.
Our hypothesis is that it caused more friction by the surprise of seeing their own name. The other possibility is that it made the Congratulations more noticeable and led them to believe their work on the page was complete and they did a good job and could now leave the page. We are now testing a form design on this page rather than a personalization, to see if making the choices in giving more clear and the process easier will increase donor conversion.

experiments.title: How including author information affects video series sign up rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Including information on the author/speaker will increase signups and email acquisition.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/26/2021 - 05/03/2021
Focus on the Family was releasing a new video series and they wanted to see what the impact of including information on the speaker would be on email conversion. The thought was that the broad audience that lands on the sign up page may not recognize the name of the speaker and so knowing more about them would convince the readers to sign up. This was a 50/50 split test and the primary traffic was from social media.
(N.B. The client set up the variable page and because of the nature of the test set up, the variable shows as treatment.)
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment did not affect conversion rate with any valid level of confidence either way. However, we do not think that means including information for the speaker is not helpful to convincing the reader to sign up. We do think that the placing the information below the decision point of the submission button made the information a second thought and so did not have an impact.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition to a premium offer impacted conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that expanding the value proposition for landing page visitors will achieve an increased conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/21/2019 - 12/16/2019
For the month of December, a special premium was being offered with a membership purchase. When evaluating the landing page copy, we wanted to develop a treatment that would increase conversions to the membership page. The control jumped right into the details about the premium and membership without giving a clear motivation for considering a Club Membership. We expanded the value proposition with a paragraph to enhance the value-add before launching into the and details of the Club benefits and premium information.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Since this was a limited time offer, we only had a short window to direct traffic to the treatment landing page. That being said, the experiment did not receive enough traffic to validate fully, but we did find that mobile visitors had a 20.1% increase in outbound link click with a 99% level of confidence. We'll continue to find opportunities to introduce similar language on other landing pages that have a longer life span and see if we can observe a valid lift on conversions in the future on other premium offers.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Tablet visitors had a 60.6% decrease in outbound link click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 23.6% decrease in outbound link click with an 90% level of confidence.
 	Social visitors had a 131.4% increase in outbound link click with an 86% level of confidence.

Focus’s video series Raising Your Kids to Defend Their Faith had a low conversion rate. So we tested placing testimonials from parents who used the series on the page. This ran for over a month. Traffic was primarily from acquisition ads on social media.

However, we do not think that means testimonials are not helpful to convincing the reader to sign up. We do think that this shows that this placement and these testimonials on this offer did not move the needle for readers. Further testing of placement and copy is needed.

experiments.title: How increasing clarity that a content offer is free affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making it clear a content offer is free, while tying that to value proposition, will not decrease giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/16/2021 - 05/20/2021
Focus on the Family had been receiving complaints that people didn’t know the video series was free and they believed they needed to make a donation to get the series. We tested one iteration of a donation page that increased the use of the word free in conjunction with the video series. That test decreased donations by 20%.
For the second iteration, we focused more on clarity and addressing the reader specifically, rather than increasing the reference to the series being free. You can see the enhanced test language below. This was a 50/50 A/B test that ran for four weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings: This iteration of the test didn't decrease donations by any level of confidence while it increased clarity that the reader did not have to donate to receive the video series. We will be rolling this language out to their other instant donation pages.

experiments.title: How a form on the right side of the page affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: A right side form could harm conversion rates
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/30/2021 - 06/04/2021
Focus’s web team wanted to roll out a new landing page format with the form on the right side of the page instead of the bottom. We wanted to assure that this did no harm to email acquisition. So we tested this on a new offer they were launching called Preparing Your Kids for the Teen Years.
experiments.keyLearnings: The majority of the traffic and conversions were on a mobile device. This test showed no results because on a mobile device it is almost exactly the same user experience. So we are going to retest this format on a high traffic page to only desktop users.

experiments.title: How a new testimonial impacted clicks on a sticky bar

rows.name: Sticky Bar Free Trial w/ Testimonial

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that demonstrating tangible outcomes in the value proposition for parents considering a free trial will achieve an increase in free trial signups.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/02/2021 - 03/06/2021
When reviewing surveys from people who had completed a 14-day trial, we found a testimonial that indicated the different skills their children built as a result of listening to Adventures in Odyssey episodes. We decided to include this testimonial as a form of social proof in the sticky bar to see if tangible outcomes in life skill areas such as “conflict resolution, compassion and addressing fears” would be motivating for parents considering beginning a free trial for their children. We split traffic between this treatment and the currently running testimonial that was implemented as the control after it had lifted results in a prior experiment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We ran the experiment for a month, but we did not validate this test. While we saw varying results across multiple segments, only a few individual segments did validate. We'll look further into the mobile vs. desktop experience for future tests since it looked like these segments had opposing effects. We can also look into adding variable copy for segments like paid search visitors on any additional treatments.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Direct Traffic visitors had a 41.2% increase in begin free trial signup with an 87% level of confidence.Paid Search visitors had a 20.5% decrease in begin free trial signup with a 97% level of confidence.Social visitors had a 2.7% increase in begin free trial signup with a 92% level of confidence.Desktop visitors had a 20.4% increase in begin free trial signup with a 96% level of confidence.Mobile visitors had a 14.3% decrease in begin free trial signup with a 94% level of confidence.(Other) visitors had a 107.0% increase in begin free trial signup with a 91% level of confidence.Direct visitors had a 36.8% increase in begin free trial signup with a 96% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a dynamic donor countdown on a sticky bar impacts conversion during a campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase donor motivation at the end of a campaign by adding a sticky bar to the donation page to reinforce the value prop?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/27/2020 - 08/04/2020
In the final week of Focus on the Family’s summer campaign, they wanted to see if they would increase donor conversion on the donation page by adding a sticky bar to the top of the page that reinforce the need. On this sticky bar was also a dynamic countdown of how many donors were needed that day (this changed every day). They ran this test for a week against the control that didn’t have the sticky bar on it.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we weren’t able to validate the results, we did see a directional lift in giving when the sticky bar was present on the page. The sticky bar increased motivation of the prospective donor by reminding them of the need and creating urgency around the goal of the number of donors needed to meet the need. This tactic has proven to work well for this organization and could be implemented in future campaigns without having a negative impact on giving.

experiments.title: How using social proof on a website can impact donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Can social proof help increase donor conversion on the FOTF website?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/17/2020 - 08/07/2020
During Focus on the Family’s summer campaign focused around their pro-life initiative, we wanted to see if we could impact donor conversion by using social proof to motivate people to give. To test this, we developed a slide in feature that appeared when a person visited the FOTF website that said, “[INSERT A PERSON’S NAME], from [INSERT CITY], made a donation to help save a child from abortion. You can help too>>”. The feature would show for 20 seconds and then disappear, but then reappear and refresh the name and city when/if someone on the website made a gift. We showed this feature on desktop and mobile devices.
experiments.keyLearnings: We weren’t able to validate the results, but we did see a directional lift (46%) in donor conversion when the social proof feature was present. We also saw the most significant increase occur on desktop devices and returning visitors. The great news is that this feature didn’t have a negative impact, even on mobile. What we can learn is that people can be impacted and motivated by others. Seeing that someone on the site had just made a gift to save a baby from abortion motivated others to do the same. We’d like to continue to test into this principle, run it for a longer period of time, and experiment with the kind of language used to see if we can validate the results and even increase donor conversion even more through this tactic.

experiments.title: How removing a video affects email appeal revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing the video from the landing page (and thumbnail from the email) increase donation revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/27/2017 - 12/04/2017
We saw a number of different factors and potential learnings at play this test.
First off, eliminating the softer CTA (“Watch Video”) in the email reduced the open-to-click rate by 34.9% in the non-video treatment.  However, there wasn’t a corresponding reduction in donations—likely suggesting that the larger audience clicking the control was less motivated to give and more motivated to watch the video.
At first glance when reviewing overall giving, we saw a 2.1% increase in conversions for the non-video treatment . . . but at a 28.8% decrease in revenue. Neither met our 95% confidence threshold, although the revenue decrease had a confidence of 90.9%.  For this reason, we scrutinized the data more closely to ensure that we didn’t miss a learning.
After reviewing the individual transactions from both variants, we found that the gift amounts had significant outliers due to $,1,000+ donations.  Knowing when to filter data is a difficult challenge of testing and optimization—but since we have repeatedly seen that higher-value gifts are less likely to be motivated by an individual element of a broad-base ask, we typically exclude these from revenue validation.  After making this adjustment, we see the smaller difference noted in the results table.
Here are a few thoughts on this result, and why it requires additional testing to validate:
Response sizes are too smallDue to a low conversion rate among both target segments, there isn’t enough data to back up the normal distribution of gift amount due to its existing variability.  This is especially true of a non-donor segment that was tested with similar results, where conversion rate was just 0.007%.  Further testing, including during higher response windows, may improve the actionability of results.
The video wasn’t the only thing testedWhen removing the video thumbnail from the email, it was replaced with a different image, which was also rotated.  The additional call to action button was also removed instead of being modified. Copy was added in that area, but from a different testimonial changing the value being conveyed.On the landing page, the same additional testimonial copy was used, but was added much later in the copy compared to the video.  When testing video as a medium, the core value proposition of the video should be represented in text—if not, you’re also testing between different value proposition or the removal of some of the value proposition presented completely.
There were significantly more outliers in the control segmentWhile excluding $1,000+ gifts from both segments made a difference, it’s difficult to tell what the normal distribution of gifts is and whether segmentation played a factor in the results.  Typically the only ways to answer these questions are to measure historical distributions to be able identify abnormal ones, or to have a larger number of results to help balance any small number of outliers.
Due to the significant impact we’ve seen when replacing video on appeal pages, we would recommend further testing to ensure a clear and applicable learning for this audience.
experiments.keyLearnings: The team at Focus on the Family had seen experiments from other NextAfter partners suggesting that video on donation forms could actually be harming conversion.  With an appeal featuring video set to go out, they decided to test into this effect to see whether their audience would respond the same.  This particular test was against a segment of existing donors, although a non-donor segment was also tested.

experiments.title: How the call-to-action within an article offer impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which call-to-action would increase reader response on article pages?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/07/2020 - 06/13/2020
On the Focus on the Family marriage articles, they featured a video series called, Cherish. This video series was free for people to sign-up for and was presented at the bottom of every marriage article. The call-to-action on the offer was a button that would then take a person to the sign up page. We hypothesized that we might be able to increase conversion if we presented the sign-up form right there on the article. We split the traffic, 50/50.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment ran for a month and we found that there was no significant difference in name conversion between the two different call-to-actions. This was a bit surprising as we have seen that reducing friction to the acquisition process can help conversion. Because the results were a wash, we hypothesize that the offer may have been over saturated on these pages since it had been running for months prior to this experiment. We hypothesize this because of the low conversion rate on the offer. We would like to test this tactic again in the future using a new, fresh, content offer on the article pages.

experiments.title: How the kind of offer impacts the kind of donor acquired
experiments.researchQuestion: Which offer would be the best acquisition opportunity on article pages?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/19/2019 - 08/01/2019
Through a series of tests, Focus on the Family discovered that segmented GOAA offers – based off of what people are consuming on the Focus website – results in the best conversion from article pages. That said, we were curious if the GOAA offer was the best opportunity to present on the article pages. To test this, we proposed testing the free video series content offer that also had a donation ask at the end of the name acquisition funnel against the GOAA offers.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment only ran for ten days and we saw no difference in donor conversion between the two offers. As we dug into the data a bit more, we learned a couple of valuable insights…
Our primary metric is donations and revenue, but what is not communicated in these results is that the video series also allowed us to acquire 472 emails addresses in addition to the donations and revenue. These emails also provide an opportunity for downstream donor conversion which isn’t measured from this experiment.
While donor conversion was not impacted by the kind of offer, revenue was. The average gift on the video series offer was significantly higher than the GOAA offer. In fact, revenue increased by over 600% with the video series offer. What this tells us is that we are able to acquire donors that have a higher value to the organization through the video series, and we are able to acquire “purchasers” through the GOAA offers. People viewed the GOAA offers as transactional, whereas the video series as an investment in the ministry and lives of other families like theirs.
The video series is the best method to acquire donors for the organization on the article pages. They provide the opportunity to not only acquire high value donors for the organization, but capture emails as well that could convert to donor downstream.

experiments.title: How removing one of the payment plans can impact member conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase member conversion by removing one of the payment plans?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/14/2019
Adventures in Odyssey offer people three different payment/membership plans. They wanted to simplify their membership process and hypothesized that if they removed one of them, they may be able to increase memberships, and ultimately overall revenue. To test into this, we removed the 6 month payment option during the second step of the membership process. We kept the 1 month and 12 month options for the treatment and split the traffic in half to test our approach
experiments.keyLearnings: After running this for a month, we found that there was no significant difference in conversion. We had hypothesized that we might be able to minimize user decision friction by removing one of the options, but we learned that removing the six month option had no impact on conversion. We’d like to test into this more and see if we can eliminate decision friction in another way by emphasizing the most popular option.

experiments.title: How clarifying the steps of a donation form impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will modifying the form headers of the donation form improve donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/19/2019 - 08/16/2019
The monthly recurring donor page for Focus on the Family had held a relatively low conversion rate when compared to other donation pages. After running more than five experiments, we had not been able to statistically improve overall results. After testing several value propositions, advertising to the page, and even adding additional functionality (live chat), we decided to shift focus to the form itself.
Based upon learnings we had seen with similar organizations, we decided to try modifying the form headers to add clarity on the purpose of each section. We also numbered the headlines to help move the user down the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for more than a month, we found that the numbered form headers improved donor conversion by 27%. The additional clarity of the headlines helped reduce the friction associated with the form. There is also potential that the mention of the form security in the final section helped to reduce anxiety as well.
The potential impact of this experiment can extend beyond just the Friend of Focus form. It would be our recommendation to test these changes on the main donation form and all “gift of any amount” forms.

experiments.title: How adding a premium to the donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would a premium on the donation page increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/09/2019 - 07/11/2019
Focus on the Family was running a paid acquisition campaign through Facebook promoting a video series on intimacy in marriage. In a previous experiment for a different video series about parenting, they found that adding a premium offer on the donation page increased donor conversion. They wanted to test this approach with this marriage video series as well to see if they’d get the same/similar results. To test this, they added a premium to the donation page that people would see after they signed up for the free video series. The premium was the “5 Love Languages” book. They also added some copy about the premium to communicate the value of it to the end reader.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we weren’t able to validate the experiment, we did see a directional lift of 18%. The results of this experiment are in line with what we saw from the previous experiment with the parenting video series. We would propose rolling this approach out to future acquisition efforts. The premium adds incentive to convert more new names into donors. What is important to note is that the content of the premium offer reinforced the content of the video series they just signed up for. The premium becomes the next logical step in the conversion process for people looking to improve their marriage.

experiments.title: How isolating one high-performing content offer over segmented offers impacts conversion on parenting articles
experiments.researchQuestion: Would the best performing segmented offer increase conversion if offered as a single offer?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/20/2019 - 06/11/2019
Through a series of tests on the Focus on the Family parenting articles, we found that presenting segmented GOAA offers based on what people consume on the Focus website increases donor conversion over one single offer. That said, we found that there is one specific GOAA offer that performed best out of all of the segmented offers presented. To isolate this single offer and see if it could increase donor conversion alone, we tested it against the segmented offers.
experiments.keyLearnings: We weren’t able to validate the results. We found that the best performing segmented offer could not impact donor conversion on it’s own. We propose moving forward with segmented offers for all traffic.

experiments.title: How presenting segmented offers based on a person’s engagement on marriage articles impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will offering people targeted premium offers increase donor conversion on marriage articles?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/15/2019 - 03/26/2019
Focus on the Family has done significant testing within the articles on their website. From testing the kind of offer to the placement of the offers, they have optimized and continue to optimize the conversion opportunities on the articles pages for the significant volume of traffic. Most recently they learned that presenting people a premium/book at the end of the articles, that readers could receive by giving a gift of any amount, had the most significant impact on donor conversion. To continue to test into this learning, they hypothesized that presenting people with a targeted offer based on their engagement on the Focus on the Family website might increase donor conversion. Focus identified the top premium offers based on what is most requested and the kind of content that people read and consume from their site and offered premiums that closely aligned with those topics. This approach was tested against offering people one single premium offer.
experiments.keyLearnings:
As we’ve continued to monitor and dig into the results of this experiment, we have discovered an interesting finding that is misrepresenting the results and learnings. A significant portion of the traffic on the article pages are either first time visitors to the Focus website, or this is the first article they’ve consumed from Focus. This audience has not been receiving targeted content offers based on cookies. Because of this, 69% of the donations from the treatment results have been receiving the single content offer/premium.
After discovering this and learning what was happening, we have paused the current experiment and relaunching it to target that audience with targeted content offers based on the current article they are reading. This small nuance to the experiment is critical in knowing whether a targeted content offer based on the kind of content people consume helps increase donor conversion.

experiments.title: How the experience on a donation confirmation page impacts subsequent gifts and overall revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase the number of subsequent gifts and overall revenue by improving the experience on the donation confirmation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/11/2019 - 05/15/2019
A lot of focus gets put on the optimization and improvement of the actual donation process and little attention is put on the experience after a person makes a gift. We hypothesized that improving this experience and making the donor feel valued, celebrated, and affirmed in their decision to make a gift to Focus on the Family we might be able to impact and improve subsequent giving and overall revenue from the donor in the future. To do this, we developed a treatment experience on the donation confirmation page that thanked the donor by name, celebrated the donor in the copy we used, reinforced the value proposition of their gift by sharing two testimonials, and even had confetti explode on the page as soon as the donor landed on the page. We tested this approach for over six months to really assess the impact that we might be able to have on giving over a longer period of time. Half of the audience during those six months experienced the control confirmation page and the other half experienced the “Super, Happy, Thankful” treatment experience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
To analyze the winner, we looked at donors based upon the initial treatment they saw and removed any recurring donations since they were automated. We then looked at the likelihood of a donor to make a subsequent gift, the number of subsequent gifts, and their change in revenue (did their subsequent giving increase). In all of those situations, there was no statistically significant difference between the control and treatment.
Even though we didn’t see an impact on future gifts and revenue, the treatment did a better job in thanking and celebrating the donor – which is important. Maybe the one joyful, celebratory experience on the donation confirmation page wasn’t enough to impact giving, but could we create that same experience in a series of emails every single month for the donor and have an impact on giving? We’d like to test it.

experiments.title: How shorter value proposition on a donation page during year-end impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would a shorter value proposition increase donor conversion for email traffic?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2018 - 01/04/2019
During Focus on the Family’s year-end campaign, we hypothesized we might be able to increase donor conversion on the donation page for the people coming to it from email. To do this, we developed a shorter value proposition that didn’t remove the strength of the message, but just made the copy/content shorter.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we weren’t able to validate this experiment, we did start to see a lift in conversion. Our hypothesis is that this traffic has already read and understands (through their email) the value proposition of the ask – they don’t need as much convincing and motivation to convert. The longer copy of the control may have even acted as friction in the giving process for these people.

experiments.title: How utilizing a longer funnel during early year end fundraising impacts giving
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the video series offer result in more donations than a direct donation ask during year end?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/30/2018 - 12/21/2018
During the early part of December, Focus on the Family had found in previous years that the direct ask banner ads did not perform well with mobile visitors. During 2018’s year end campaign, they decided to test a tactic of presenting an offer for their new online video series. It is an email acquisition offer that presented new subscribers with a donation opportunity once they had finished the signup process. They launched this offer against their standard direct donation ask that runs throughout all of December.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We did not reach a level of statistical significance with this experiment. We saw a difference of 12 gifts between the control and treatment. However, it is worth noting that the video series resulted in nearly 400 emails acquired for the organization. These emails have the potential to convert downstream and provide additional value.
In future campaigns, it may be worth testing an offer that is a middle-ground between an email acquisition and direct donation ask. For Focus, that would be a “gift of any amount” offer that offers a book or DVD when they make a donation.

experiments.title: How a “forwarded” email impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will a forward email impact conversion?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/28/2019
Focus on the Family ran a year end campaign in 2018. They hypothesized that sending an email that looked like a “FWD” would increase engagement and conversion. They decided to test this against an email with a subject line that didn’t have “FWD” in it.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment is not valid because the level of confidence is only 51.2%. Having a forwarded email has no impact on conversion. The reason for this is because motivation is so high of the year. In the context of a year end campaign, a forwarded email has no impact on donor conversion.

experiments.title: How using exclusivity on a recurring gift page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will donor conversion increase by appealing to potential recurring donors through exclusivity?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/31/2019 - 02/26/2019
There has been significant testing done on Focus on the Family’s recurring gift donation page. The majority of this testing has been around strengthening the value proposition. We hypothesized that increasing the exclusivity on the page might increase donor conversion. To do this, we created a treatment that was more conversational and tried to appeal to recurring donors as a select, exclusive group of people, “the few”, that have been chosen or stepped up to give on an ongoing basis.
experiments.keyLearnings: The experiment ran for over a month and we weren’t able to validate the results. The treatment had neither a positive or negative impact on donor conversion. When we analyze the experiment, we see that the donor conversion is really low on the page. What this tells us is that we would need a more radical version of the page to impact conversion, or look at testing higher in the funnel to get MORE people to the recurring donor page in an effort to increase recurring donors from this page.

experiments.title: How using altruistic language for the benefits of a recurring gift impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will altruistic language on the recurring gift page impact donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/27/2018 - 01/23/2019
We had previously tested using altruistic language on the recurring gift pop-up but we weren’t able to impact donor conversion positively or negatively with it. Understanding this we hypothesized that the altruistic language might have a greater impact on the actual recurring page where the motivation isn’t quite as high and we might be able to impact donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment ran for eight weeks and we weren’t able to validate it. What this tells us is that the altruistic language really had no impact on conversion. We hypothesize that the majority of people aren’t getting to the part of the page that communicates the altruistic benefits. Because of this, we propose a more radical experiment for the page that tests the overall value proposition.

experiments.title: How presenting a gift of any amount instead of a direct ask impacts donor conversion on mobile devices.
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase donor conversion on mobile devices during year-end by presenting a gift of any amount over a direct ask?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2018 - 01/02/2019
During Focus on the Family’s year-end campaign, we hypothesized that we might be able to increase donor conversion on the homepage of mobile devices if we presented people with a relevant premium offer for a gift of any amount instead of a direct year-end donation ask. We featured a devotional book for the treatment that included an image of the book and the appeal. When people clicked through to the donation page, there was an appeal for year-end for a gift of any amount to get the book.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment didn’t validate and we saw little to no difference in donor conversion with the treatment. This is due to the motivation of donors during this point in the campaign. The experiment was launched the week prior to the end of the campaign when motivation is much higher. We propose testing this approach earlier in the campaign and even during the normal giving season to see the impact on donor conversion.

experiments.title: How presenting a gift of any amount instead of a direct ask impacts donor conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion: Would presenting people with a content offer for a gift of any amount instead of a direct donation ask impact donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2018 - 01/02/2019
During Focus on the Family’s year-end campaign, we hypothesized that we might be able to increase donor conversion on the homepage if we presented people with a relevant premium offer for a gift of any amount instead of a direct year-end donation ask. We featured a devotional book for the treatment that included an image of the book and the appeal. When people clicked through to the donation page, there was an appeal for year-end for a gift of any amount to get the book.
experiments.keyLearnings: The GOAA offer had little to no impact on donor conversion. This can be attributed to the motivation of users during the time the experiment was launched. We propose testing this approach again earlier in the campaign when donor motivation is more neutral.

experiments.title: How an expanded value proposition on a pledge donation page affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Would expanding the value proposition increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/13/2018 - 03/15/2018
Focus on the Family has a separate donation page from their primary page specifically for recurring giving. When looking at the copy on this page, we hypothesized that the copy led with a more transactional nature and that the messaging could be strengthened to better communicate the impact a person could have by becoming a recurring donor. We developed a treatment that reinforced the value proposition.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for a month, we weren’t able to validate the results. This tells us that the expanded value proposition had no impact on conversion. Conversion rate on this page is very low. We will need to do a more radical redesign and address multiple friction points on the page if we want to decrease anxiety and increase motivation of people on the page.

experiments.title: How addressing a person’s fear to become a recurring donor impacts conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion: Would addressing a person’s potential fear of not being able to cancel their recurring gift increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/23/2018 - 05/09/2018
On Focus on the Family’s recurring gift donation page, we hypothesized that people may have a fear of commitment when it came to becoming a recurring donor. To address this potential concern, we added copy to the page that clearly communicated to people that they could cancel their recurring gift at any time.
experiments.keyLearnings: We had hypothesized that addressing a person’s fear in becoming a recurring donor could dramatically impact donor conversion. What we found was that addressing this element of fear had no impact on conversion. This tells us that there is a different element of friction causing the low conversion rates. We propose potentially discovering this through an exit-intent survey asking users why they didn’t become a recurring donor.

experiments.title: How strengthening the value proposition on the Plugged In donation page impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would an expanded and strengthened value proposition increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/04/2018 - 05/22/2018
On Focus on the Family’s Plugged In donation page, we hypothesized that we might be able to increase donor conversion on that page by adding additional value proposition for why a person should give and what their donation would help accomplish. We developed a treatment that not only strengthened the value proposition, but also connected Plugged In with the parent brand, Focus on the Family. We included Plugged In as an evidential for why a person should support Focus on the Family.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running this experiment for three weeks, we saw no significant difference in conversion with the treatment. In looking at the data from this experiment, a clear insight from it is that the conversion rate on the page is extremely low. The low conversion rate points to low motivation of the end user. Not even strengthening the value proposition were we able to impact the motivation of the end user. The next approach in testing this page needs to be focused on a more radical treatment to better understand the motivation and kind of people visiting this page.

experiments.title: How making the daily resource less transactional on Adventures in Odyssey impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which offer increased conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/08/2018 - 03/08/2018
The Adventures in Odyssey page gets significant traffic. In an effort to turn this traffic into donors, we hypothesized that turning the daily resource offer into something less transactional might increase conversion. To do this, we developed a treatment that told people the kind of impact they would have when they gave to Focus on the Family, and specifically when they supported Adventures in Odyssey. We also offered them a free 90 Day Devotional for Kids as an incentive for their gift.
experiments.keyLearnings: Because of the low conversion on this page, there was not enough data to validate or see a significant difference between the two offers. The majority of the traffic on this page is returning visitors. The motivation of people on this page is to get the audio. Knowing that, we will give them something of value instead of asking them to give and get to see if we can increase conversion on the page.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition on the primary donation page impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How would adding value proposition to the primary donation page impact conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/02/2018 - 02/27/2018
In an effort to increase donor conversion on the Focus on the Family primary donation page, we hypothesized that adding value proposition to it would increase donor motivation and conversion. To do this, we developed a value proposition for the page that communicated why a person should give to Focus and what a person’s gift would go towards. We then tested this treatment page against the control.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for four weeks, we turned it off because we were seeing minimal impact at a low level of confidence. Because the main donation page has high motivation of people, the additional value proposition isn’t impacting conversion.
When we looked at conversion based on specific devices, we noticed on mobile that the updated value proposition had a negative conversion. We would hypothesize this is due to the additional length that came with the copy on the treatment.
We propose continuing to test the value proposition on the primary donation page, but decreasing the length of the copy on the treatment. This would help to validate the hypothesis on copy length and the impact a strong value proposition can have.

experiments.title: How expanding a sub-brand’s value proposition to include the broader ministry brand impacts conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase conversion by expanding the value proposition on the page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/17/2018 - 02/20/2018
On the Plugged In donation page we hypothesized that expanding the value proposition might help increase conversion on the page. We proposed testing two versions of the value proposition on the donation page; the current Plugged In donation page focuses on that particular sub-brand and the impact the program has. As a treatment, we developed a message around the broader Focus on the Family brand and value proposition. We then included language about Plugged In in that treatment copy but only as evidentials for how Focus is accomplishing the broader mission.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for a month, we weren’t able reach a significant level of confidence with the results (20%). We had trouble validating this experiment, not due to the sample size, but because the conversion rate on the page is so low. This tell us that the people coming to this page are not coming to give or are ready to give. No amount of value proposition will help increase the conversion rate on this specific page when we are asking for a gift. Instead, we need to be testing something higher in the conversion funnel. We propose testing a content offer on this page that then leads into a soft donation ask.

experiments.title: How removing an exit-intent pop-up on a donation page impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing the exit-intent pop-up impact conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/02/2018 - 01/26/2018
On Focus on the Family’s primary donation page, they use an exit-intent pop-up that appears on the page as you are about to leave. During their year-end giving campaign, we tested removing this feature. We found that by removing the exit-intent pop-up, we were able to increase donor conversion because the audience was highly motivated to give. Understanding these learnings, we also wanted to test this approach outside of year-end when motivations were more neutral. During the month of January, we tested removing the exit-intent pop-up from the primary donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After four weeks of running this experiment and analyzing the data, we found that having an exit-intent pop-up on the primary donation page doesn’t significantly impact conversion – positively or negatively outside of high-urgency campaigns. The conversion rate throughout the month fluctuated week by week telling us that there is no real difference in conversion with an exit-intent pop-up.
You can see this fluctuation in the graph below where the lines cross paths multiple times.
This learning is valuable to know because of the cost associated with having the exit-intent pop-up on the site. Even though the test didn’t validate, we would recommend removing the exit-intent pop-up all together from the primary donation page – both during high urgency campaigns like year-end and during regular times of the year when motivation is neutralized.

experiments.title: How the headline value proposition affects acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: By stating what the visitor will “get” within the headline, we can improve the conversion rate on the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/26/2014 - 12/14/2014
The audience was already highly motivated and the ads had conveyed what would be received on the subsequent page. As a result, the headlines did not produce a significant lift to conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
As part of the calendar year end campaign, DTS produced an eBook that they gave away free to their supporters. To expand the impact of this free offer, we also created landing pages that were advertised to Facebook audiences. These pages offered the book for the price of an email address.
With the influx of traffic, we used the opportunity to test the impact that a headline can have on conveying the value proposition of the page.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign for a year-end donation page impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would a radical redesign impacts conversion on the year-end donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2017 - 12/27/2017
During the Focus on the Family year-end campaign, we hypothesized that we might be able increase conversion by testing their current year-end donation page with a radical redesign. The radical redesign included removing the video on the page, adding a countdown clock on the page, increasing value proposition and added a giving thermometer.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We weren’t able to validate this experiment but initial data shows us that there was no real significant difference between the control and the treatment. With such a low level of confidence we can hypothesize a couple of take aways and next steps:
The more we learn about the Focus on the Family audience, the more we are realizing that this audience has a very different motivation and we must take a different testing approach.
Normally, we can dramatically impact conversion with radical redesigns. But with the Focus on the Family audience, we want to learn more specifically about what impacts donor motivation and conversion. To do this, we need to isolate specific variables in our testing instead of doing radical redesigns.
